section_header: ICD-10-CM Official Guidelines for Coding and Reporting
description: This passage implements the updated FY 2025 ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines issued by CMS and NCHS, which define the official rules for disease classification and reporting for the period of October 1, 2024, through September 30, 2025.

qna.q: Why do these guidelines explicitly state that classification instructions take precedence over the general guidelines?
qna.a: This hierarchy exists because the Tabular List and Alphabetic Index contain specific, disease-specific conventions that override general rules to ensure clinical accuracy. If a general guideline contradicted a specific instruction for a particular condition, following the general rule could lead to incorrect coding for that specific diagnosis.

qna.q: How does the requirement for adherence to these guidelines under HIPAA impact real-world healthcare operations?
qna.a: It transforms coding from a best practice into a federal mandate, meaning non-compliance can result in legal penalties or denial of reimbursement for claims. This forces healthcare providers to integrate these specific rules directly into their electronic health record systems to avoid financial and regulatory risks.

qna.q: What does the involvement of the American Hospital Association and AHIMA imply about the nature of these updates?
qna.a: It implies that the changes are not just statistical adjustments but are vetted by industry experts who understand the practical workflow of hospitals and health information management. Their participation ensures that the new rules are feasible to implement and align with how medical data is actually recorded and managed in daily practice.

section_header: Section I.  Conventions, general coding guidelines and chapter specific guidelines
text: The conventions, general guidelines and chapter-specific guidelines are applicable to all health care settings unless otherwise indicated. The conventions and instructions of the classification take precedence over guidelines.
description: Establishes that conventions and chapter-specific guidelines apply to all care settings unless otherwise indicated, with classification instructions taking precedence over general guidelines.

qna.q: Why does the classification system explicitly state that conventions take precedence over general guidelines, and what does this imply for real-world coding consistency?
qna.a: This hierarchy ensures that rigid structural rules (conventions) override flexible best practices (guidelines) to maintain a uniform data structure across all healthcare settings. In practice, this means coders must prioritize the strict formatting or logic defined in the conventions even if a general guideline suggests a different approach, preventing data fragmentation caused by subjective interpretation.

text: The conventions for the ICD-10-CM are the general rules for use of the classification independent of the guidelines. These conventions are incorporated within the Alphabetic Index and Tabular List of the ICD-10-CM as instructional notes.
description: Defines the general rules for using the ICD-10-CM classification system that are independent of specific guidelines. These conventions are embedded as instructional notes within the Alphabetic Index and Tabular List.

text:
The ICD-10-CM Tabular List contains categories, subcategories and codes. Characters for categories, subcategories and codes may be either a letter or a number. All categories are 3 characters. A three-character category that has no further subdivision is equivalent to a code. Subcategories are either 4 or 5 characters. Codes may be 3, 4, 5, 6 or 7 characters. That is, each level of subdivision after a category is a subcategory. The final level of subdivision is a code. Codes that have applicable 7 th characters are still referred to as codes, not subcategories. A code that has an applicable 7 th character is considered invalid without the 7 th character.

The ICD-10-CM uses an indented format for ease in reference.
description: Defines the hierarchical structure of the ICD-10-CM Tabular List, specifying character lengths for categories, subcategories, and codes up to the final seventh character level.

qna.q: Why is a three-character category treated as a valid code if it has no further subdivision?
qna.a: This design allows the system to assign a definitive diagnosis immediately when clinical data is insufficient to justify deeper specificity. It prevents the need for a placeholder or null value, ensuring every encounter has a billable code even at the broadest level of granularity.

qna.q: How does the rule that a code with an applicable 7th character is invalid without it affect data processing?
qna.a: The system must enforce a strict validation step where any record containing a 7th-character modifier is rejected if that character is missing. This prevents ambiguous clinical reporting where laterality or severity details are critical for accurate treatment and reimbursement.

qna.q: What does the distinction between 'subcategories' and 'codes' imply for how we structure medical records?
qna.a: It implies that the data hierarchy is not just a list of options but a mandatory progression where a subcategory is an incomplete state requiring further refinement. A record cannot be considered fully resolved until it reaches the final 'code' level, ensuring maximum precision in the final diagnosis.

section_header: 3. Use of codes for reporting purposes
text: For reporting purposes only codes are permissible, not categories or subcategories, and any applicable 7 th character is required.
description: Specifies that only permissible codes are allowed for reporting purposes, excluding categories and subcategories, while mandating the inclusion of the seventh character.

text: The ICD-10-CM utilizes a placeholder character 'X'. The 'X' is used as a placeholder at certain codes to allow for future expansion. An example of this is at the poisoning, adverse effect and underdosing codes, categories T36-T50. Where a placeholder exists, the X must be used in order for the code to be considered a valid code.
description: The ICD-10-CM system utilizes the placeholder character 'X' within categories T36-T50 to enable future expansion for poisoning and adverse effect codes. This character is mandatory for the code to be considered valid in these specific categories.

qna.q: Why does the ICD-10-CM system mandate the use of 'X' as a placeholder in categories like T36-T50 instead of leaving the character blank or using a different symbol?
qna.a: The 'X' acts as a structural delimiter that ensures the code maintains a consistent seven-character length, which is critical for automated data processing and sorting algorithms. Without this fixed-length requirement, legacy systems and modern databases would struggle to parse codes correctly, leading to errors in billing, statistical reporting, and clinical data aggregation.

text: Certain ICD-10-CM categories have applicable 7 th characters. The applicable 7 th character is required for all codes within the category, or as the notes in the Tabular List instruct. The 7 th character must always be the 7 th character in the data field. If a code that requires a 7 th character is not 6 characters, a placeholder X must be used to fill in the empty characters.
description: Validates that required 7th characters are correctly positioned and appended to ICD-10-CM codes, using placeholder 'X' for codes shorter than six characters.

qna.q: Why is a placeholder 'X' required to fill empty characters before the 7th position instead of simply skipping to the 7th slot?
qna.a: This mechanism ensures the data field maintains a fixed length and strict positional integrity, which is critical for database indexing and automated parsing. If the system allowed skipping characters, a code requiring a 7th character could be misaligned with codes that don't, leading to parsing errors where the 7th character is read as the 6th or 5th.

text:
NEC 'Not elsewhere classifiable'

This abbreviation in the Tabular List represents 'other specified'. When a specific code is not available for a condition, the Tabular List includes an NEC entry under a code to identify the code as the 'other specified' code.

NOS 'Not otherwise specified'

This abbreviation is the equivalent of unspecified.
description: Defines the NEC and NOS abbreviations within the Tabular List to represent 'not elsewhere classifiable' and 'not otherwise specified' conditions.

qna.q: Why does the Tabular List distinguish between NEC ('Not elsewhere classifiable') and NOS ('Not otherwise specified') rather than treating them as synonyms for missing data?
qna.a: The distinction exists because NEC implies a specific condition exists but lacks a dedicated code, whereas NOS indicates the condition itself is undefined or unknown. This differentiation is critical for clinical coding accuracy, as NEC entries allow for precise categorization of known conditions, while NOS entries flag cases where the diagnosis is too vague to be coded specifically.

text:
- [ ] Brackets are used in the Tabular List to enclose synonyms, alternative wording or explanatory phrases. Brackets are used in the Alphabetic Index to identify manifestation codes.
- ( ) Parentheses are used in both the Alphabetic Index and Tabular List to enclose supplementary words that may be present or absent in the statement of a disease or procedure without affecting the code number to which it is assigned. The terms within the parentheses are referred to as nonessential modifiers. The nonessential modifiers in the Alphabetic Index to Diseases apply to subterms following a main term except when a nonessential modifier and a subentry are mutually exclusive, the subentry takes precedence. For example, in the ICD-10-CM Alphabetic Index under the main term Enteritis, 'acute' is a nonessential modifier and 'chronic' is a subentry. In this case, the nonessential modifier 'acute' does not apply to the subentry 'chronic'.

- : Colons are used in the Tabular List after an incomplete term which needs one or more of the modifiers following the colon to make it assignable to a given category.
description: Defines the usage of brackets and parentheses within the ICD-10-CM Tabular List and Alphabetic Index to distinguish between synonyms and nonessential modifiers.

qna.q: Why does the system distinguish between brackets and parentheses when assigning maternal and newborn codes?
qna.a: Brackets signal synonyms or explanatory text that do not change the code, while parentheses indicate nonessential modifiers that can be ignored unless they conflict with specific subentries. This distinction ensures coders don't mistakenly treat optional descriptors as mandatory criteria for code selection.

qna.q: How does the rule regarding mutually exclusive subentries prevent coding errors in conditions like chronic enteritis?
qna.a: The mechanism prioritizes specific subentries (like 'chronic') over general nonessential modifiers (like 'acute') found in the main term. This prevents the system from incorrectly applying the acute descriptor to a chronic condition, ensuring the code reflects the actual disease severity.

qna.q: What does the use of colons imply about the structure of incomplete terms in the Tabular List?
qna.a: Colons indicate that a term is incomplete and requires additional modifiers from the list immediately following the colon to become assignable. This implies that without selecting one of those specific modifiers, the term cannot be mapped to a valid code category.

text: Codes titled 'other' or 'other specified' are for use when the information in the medical record provides detail for which a specific code does not exist. Alphabetic Index entries with NEC in the line designate 'other' codes in the Tabular List. These Alphabetic Index entries represent specific disease entities for which no specific code exists, so the term is included within an 'other' code.
master_label: ICD Code Designation and NEC Index Logic
description: Defines the use of 'other' or 'other specified' codes when specific disease entities lack a dedicated code, utilizing NEC line entries in the Alphabetic Index to designate these categories in the Tabular List.

qna.q: Why does the system require an Alphabetic Index entry with 'NEC' to designate an 'other' code rather than allowing coders to select 'other' codes freely?
qna.a: This mechanism ensures that 'other' codes are only used when a specific disease entity exists in the index but lacks a dedicated code, preventing the misuse of these codes for conditions that actually have specific definitions. By restricting selection to explicit 'NEC' entries, the system enforces a strict hierarchy where coders must first confirm no specific code exists before applying a generic 'other' designation.

text:
Codes titled 'unspecified' are for use when the information in the medical record is insufficient to assign a more specific code. For those categories for which an unspecified code is not provided, the 'other specified' code may represent both other and unspecified.

See Section I.B.18. Use of Signs/Symptom/Unspecified Codes
description: Defines the use of unspecified codes when medical record information is insufficient for specific classification. It clarifies that unspecified codes are reserved for cases where more detailed data is unavailable.

qna.q: Why does the guideline allow 'other specified' codes to represent both 'other' and 'unspecified' categories when a specific code is missing?
qna.a: This flexibility exists to prevent clinical documentation gaps from halting billing or data analysis entirely when a precise diagnosis cannot be confirmed. By allowing 'other specified' to cover unspecified scenarios, the system ensures that valid medical encounters are still captured and reimbursed even if the provider lacks the specific details required for a granular code.

text: This note appears immediately under a three-character code title to further define, or give examples of, the content of the category.
description: This implementation adds a descriptive note field positioned directly beneath three-character code titles to provide specific definitions or illustrative examples for the associated category content.

qna.q: Why is this note positioned immediately under the three-character code title rather than in a separate documentation section?
qna.a: Placing the definition directly beneath the code creates an immediate contextual link, ensuring that users understand the specific meaning of the code before they even begin interpreting the data. This proximity reduces cognitive load by preventing the user from having to cross-reference external documentation to validate their initial assumption about the category.

text: List of terms is included under some codes. These terms are the conditions for which that code is to be used. The terms may be synonyms of the code title, or, in the case of 'other specified' codes, the terms are a list of the various conditions assigned to that code. The inclusion terms are not necessarily exhaustive. Additional terms found only in the Alphabetic Index may also be assigned to a code.
master_label: ICD-10 Code Inclusion and Index Assignment
description: Defines the list of terms, including synonyms and specific conditions, that are assigned to a code for proper usage. It clarifies that inclusion terms may not be exhaustive and can include additional entries found in the Alphabetic Index.

qna.q: Why are inclusion terms described as 'not necessarily exhaustive' and how does this affect real-world coding accuracy?
qna.a: This limitation exists because clinical documentation often evolves or uses specific phrasing not yet captured in the standard list, requiring coders to cross-reference the Alphabetic Index for additional valid terms. This mechanism ensures that valid diagnoses aren't missed simply because a synonym wasn't pre-listed, but it demands a rigorous two-step verification process to prevent assigning codes based on incomplete data.

text: The ICD-10-CM has two types of excludes notes. Each type of note has a different definition for use, but they are all similar in that they indicate that codes excluded from each other are independent of each other.
description: Defines the two distinct types of excludes notes used in ICD-10-CM to indicate that excluded codes are independent of one another.

qna.q: Why does the system distinguish between two types of excludes notes if they both indicate codes are independent?
qna.a: The distinction exists because one type is mandatory, requiring the coder to explicitly choose between conflicting codes, while the other is informational, allowing both codes to be used together if clinically appropriate. This mechanism prevents coding errors where a coder might incorrectly assume an 'excludes' note forbids the use of a related code, ensuring accurate representation of complex patient conditions.

text:
A type 1 Excludes note is a pure excludes note. It means 'NOT CODED HERE!' An Excludes1 note indicates that the code excluded should never be used at the same time as the code above the Excludes1 note. An Excludes1 is used when two conditions cannot occur together, such as a congenital form versus an acquired form of the same condition.

An exception to the Excludes1 definition is the circumstance when the two conditions are unrelated to each other. If it is not clear whether the two conditions involving an Excludes1 note are related or not, query the provider. For example, code F45.8, Other somatoform disorders, has an Excludes1 note for "sleep related teeth grinding (G47.63)," because "teeth grinding" is an inclusion term under F45.8. Only one of these two codes should be assigned for teeth grinding. However psychogenic dysmenorrhea is also an inclusion term under F45.8, and a patient could have both this condition and sleep related teeth grinding. In this case, the two conditions are clearly unrelated to each other, and so it would be appropriate to report F45.8 and G47.63 together.
description: Defines the Excludes1 note as a pure exclusion indicating that two conditions cannot be coded together, such as congenital versus acquired forms. It specifies that unrelated conditions under an Excludes1 note require provider clarification before assignment.

qna.q: Why does an Excludes1 note mandate that two codes never be used together, and what real-world scenario justifies this strict exclusion?
qna.a: This restriction exists because the two conditions represent mutually exclusive states of the same disease, such as a congenital form versus an acquired form. Using both codes would create a logical contradiction in the medical record, implying the patient simultaneously has two impossible versions of the same condition.

qna.q: How does the system determine when an Excludes1 note is an exception to its usual 'never code together' rule?
qna.a: The exception applies when the two conditions are clinically unrelated, even if they share a broad inclusion term under the parent code. In these cases, the provider must be queried to confirm the lack of relationship, allowing both codes to be reported if the patient genuinely suffers from both distinct issues.

qna.q: What are the implications for coding accuracy when a provider fails to clarify whether two Excludes1 conditions are related?
qna.a: Without explicit clarification, the default assumption is that the conditions are mutually exclusive, leading to the exclusion of one code to prevent invalid data. This protects the integrity of the dataset by avoiding the assignment of contradictory diagnoses, but it may result in under-coding if the conditions were actually unrelated.

text: A type 2 Excludes note represents 'Not included here.' An excludes2 note indicates that the condition excluded is not part of the condition represented by the code, but a patient may have both conditions at the same time. When an Excludes2 note appears under a code, it is acceptable to use both the code and the excluded code together, when appropriate.
description: Implements the Excludes2 note logic to indicate that an excluded condition is not part of the primary code but may coexist with it. This allows the system to accept both the primary code and the excluded code simultaneously when clinically appropriate.

qna.q: Why does the system distinguish between 'Excludes1' and 'Excludes2' notes when mapping clinical codes, and what does this imply for data accuracy?
qna.a: The distinction exists because Excludes1 notes represent mutually exclusive conditions that cannot coexist in a single patient, whereas Excludes2 notes allow for concurrent diagnoses. This implies that the system must enforce strict logic during coding to prevent invalid combinations for Excludes1, while permitting flexible, clinically accurate pairing for Excludes2 scenarios.

section_header: 13. Etiology/manifestation convention ('code first', 'use additional code' and 'in diseases classified elsewhere' notes)
text:
Certain conditions have both an underlying etiology and multiple body system manifestations due to the underlying etiology. For such conditions, the ICD-10-CM has a coding convention that requires the underlying condition be sequenced first, if applicable, followed by the manifestation. Wherever such a combination exists, there is a 'use additional code' note at the etiology code, and a 'code first' note at the manifestation code. These instructional notes indicate the proper sequencing order of the codes, etiology followed by manifestation.

In most cases the manifestation codes will have in the code title, 'in diseases classified elsewhere.' Codes with this title are a component of the etiology/ manifestation convention. The code title indicates that it is a manifestation code. 'In diseases classified elsewhere' codes are never permitted to be used as first listed or principal diagnosis codes. They must be used in conjunction with an underlying condition code and they must be listed following the underlying condition. See category F02, Dementia in other diseases classified elsewhere, for an example of this convention.

There are manifestation codes that do not have 'in diseases classified elsewhere' in the title. For such codes, there is a 'use additional code' note at the etiology code and a 'code first' note at the manifestation code, and the rules for sequencing apply.

In addition to the notes in the Tabular List, these conditions also have a specific Alphabetic Index entry structure. In the Alphabetic Index both conditions are listed together with the etiology code first followed by the manifestation codes in brackets. The code in brackets is always to be sequenced second.

An example of the etiology/manifestation convention is dementia with Parkinson's disease. In the Alphabetic Index, a code from category G20 is listed first, followed by code F02.80 or F02.81 -in brackets. A code from category G20 -represents the underlying etiology, Parkinson's disease, and must be sequenced first, whereas codes F02.80 and F02.81 -represent the manifestation of dementia in diseases classified elsewhere, with or without behavioral disturbance.

'Code first' and 'Use additional code' notes are also used as sequencing rules in the classification for certain codes that are not part of an etiology/ manifestation combination.

See Section I.B.7. Multiple coding for a single condition.
description: Implements the ICD-10-CM coding convention requiring underlying etiology codes to be sequenced first, followed by manifestation codes with 'in diseases classified elsewhere' titles.

qna.q: Why does the ICD-10-CM convention require the underlying etiology to be sequenced before the manifestation code?
qna.a: This sequencing rule exists because the manifestation is clinically dependent on the specific underlying disease; without identifying the root cause first, the clinical picture is incomplete. The 'code first' instruction at the manifestation level enforces this logic to ensure the principal diagnosis reflects the primary pathology driving the symptoms.

qna.q: How does the presence of 'in diseases classified elsewhere' in a code title function as a mechanism to prevent coding errors?
qna.a: The title acts as a built-in flag that explicitly forbids the code from being used as a principal diagnosis, forcing the coder to look up the associated etiology. This mechanism prevents the common error of listing a symptom or secondary effect as the primary reason for admission when a specific disease is known to be the cause.

qna.q: What does the specific Alphabetic Index structure (etiology followed by manifestation in brackets) imply for real-world coding workflows?
qna.a: This structure implies that the coding process must be hierarchical: the coder must first identify and select the etiology code before the system or manual logic can validate the corresponding manifestation code. It ensures that the final claim accurately represents the causal relationship between the disease and the observed clinical signs.

text:
The word 'and' should be interpreted to mean either 'and' or 'or' when it appears in a title.

For example, cases of 'tuberculosis of bones', 'tuberculosis of joints' and 'tuberculosis of bones and joints' are classified to subcategory A18.0, Tuberculosis of bones and joints.
description: Defines the interpretation of the word 'and' in titles to classify tuberculosis cases of bones, joints, or both into subcategory A18.0.

qna.q: Why does the system interpret 'and' as 'or' in titles like 'tuberculosis of bones and joints' instead of requiring an exact match?
qna.a: This change likely addresses a data quality issue where titles are often abbreviated or simplified in source documents, omitting the conjunction while retaining the core conditions. By treating 'and' as 'or', the classifier ensures that cases listing only one of the affected body parts are still correctly routed to the combined subcategory, preventing under-classification due to missing conjunctions.

text:
The word 'with' or 'in' should be interpreted to mean 'associated with' or 'due to' when it appears in a code title, the Alphabetic Index (either under a main term or subterm), or an instructional note in the Tabular List. The classification presumes a causal relationship between the two conditions linked by these terms in the Alphabetic Index or Tabular List. These conditions should be coded as related even in the absence of provider documentation explicitly linking them, unless the documentation clearly states the conditions are unrelated or when another guideline exists that specifically requires a documented linkage between two conditions (e.g., sepsis guideline for 'acute organ dysfunction that is not clearly associated with the sepsis').

For conditions not specifically linked by these relational terms in the classification or when a guideline requires that a linkage between two conditions be explicitly documented, provider documentation must link the conditions in order to code them as related.

The word 'with' in the Alphabetic Index is sequenced immediately following the main term or subterm, not in alphabetical order.
description: Defines that terms like 'with' or 'in' in the Alphabetic Index or Tabular List presume a causal relationship between linked conditions. These conditions must be coded as related unless documentation explicitly states they are unrelated or specific guidelines require documented linkage.

qna.q: Why does the system presume a causal relationship between conditions linked by 'with' or 'in' even without explicit provider documentation?
qna.a: This presumption exists because the classification structure itself encodes clinical logic, treating these terms as definitive indicators of association rather than mere coexistence. By coding them as related automatically, the system prevents under-coding of complex conditions where the causal link is implied by the terminology but not explicitly restated in the note.

qna.q: How does the specific sequencing of the word 'with' in the Alphabetic Index affect code selection compared to standard alphabetical sorting?
qna.a: Placing 'with' immediately after the main term ensures that the system prioritizes the causal relationship as the primary search path rather than treating it as a generic modifier. This forces the coder to evaluate the specific condition pair first, ensuring that the intended causal link is captured before considering unrelated conditions that might appear alphabetically later.

qna.q: What are the real-world implications for coding accuracy when a guideline like sepsis requires explicit documentation despite the general presumption of causality?
qna.a: In these specific cases, the system must override the default presumption to prevent over-coding, as sepsis-related organ dysfunction is only coded as related if the provider explicitly confirms the link. This distinction ensures that conditions are not incorrectly bundled as complications when the clinical picture suggests they are independent or unrelated events.

text:
The 'see' instruction following a main term in the Alphabetic Index indicates that another term should be referenced. It is necessary to go to the main term referenced with the 'see' note to locate the correct code.

A 'see also' instruction following a main term in the Alphabetic Index instructs that there is another main term that may also be referenced that may provide additional Alphabetic Index entries that may be useful. It is not necessary to follow the 'see also' note when the original main term provides the necessary code.
description: Defines the logic for interpreting 'see' and 'see also' instructions within the Alphabetic Index to locate correct main terms and codes.

qna.q: Why does the system require following a 'see' instruction to find the correct code rather than providing it directly under the initial term?
qna.a: This design forces the user to navigate to the primary term where the definitive code is located, ensuring that the most specific and accurate classification is applied. It prevents ambiguity by directing users away from secondary or synonymous terms that might not carry the full coding weight required for billing or diagnosis.

qna.q: What is the practical implication of treating 'see also' instructions as optional rather than mandatory?
qna.a: It allows coders to skip the lookup if the original term already provides the necessary code, streamlining the workflow for straightforward cases. This distinction prevents unnecessary delays in coding when additional cross-references do not add value to the specific clinical scenario being documented.

text: A 'code also' note instructs that two codes may be required to fully describe a condition, but this note does not provide sequencing direction. The sequencing depends on the circumstances of the encounter.
master_label: Code Sequencing Logic for Encounter Notes
description: This logic implements conditional sequencing for 'code also' notes by deferring the order of two required codes to the specific circumstances of the encounter, as the note itself lacks inherent sequencing direction.

qna.q: Why does the 'code also' note for congenital sepsis explicitly state that sequencing depends on the circumstances of the encounter rather than providing a fixed order?
qna.a: This flexibility exists because congenital sepsis can manifest as either a primary diagnosis driving the admission or a secondary condition complicating an existing issue, requiring the coder to analyze the clinical timeline. The mechanism relies on the provider's documentation of which condition was the reason for the encounter versus which was found during the visit, ensuring the code sequence accurately reflects the medical reality rather than a rigid algorithm.

text: A code listed next to a main term in the ICD-10-CM Alphabetic Index is referred to as a default code. The default code represents that condition that is most commonly associated with the main term or is the unspecified code for the condition. If a condition is documented in a medical record (for example, appendicitis) without any additional information, such as acute or chronic, the default code should be assigned.
description: Defines the use of default codes from the ICD-10-CM Alphabetic Index when a condition is documented without additional qualifiers like acute or chronic.

qna.q: Why does the ICD-10-CM Alphabetic Index rely on a 'default code' when a condition is documented without specific qualifiers like 'acute' or 'chronic'?
qna.a: This mechanism ensures coding consistency by assigning the most statistically probable diagnosis when clinical documentation lacks specificity, preventing coders from guessing between multiple possibilities. It acts as a safety net for incomplete records, allowing the claim to be processed with a valid code rather than being rejected for insufficient detail.

text: The assignment of a diagnosis code is based on the provider's diagnostic statement that the condition exists. The provider's statement that the patient has a particular condition is sufficient. Code assignment is not based on clinical criteria used by the provider to establish the diagnosis. If there is conflicting medical record documentation, query the provider.
master_label: Code Assignment and Diagnosis Exception Logic
description: Defines that diagnosis code assignment relies solely on the provider's statement that a condition exists, without requiring additional clinical criteria. Conflicting documentation requires a provider query to resolve discrepancies.

qna.q: Why does the system prioritize the provider's explicit statement over clinical criteria when assigning diagnosis codes?
qna.a: This approach ensures legal and billing accuracy by treating the provider's documented assertion as the definitive source of truth for reimbursement purposes. It prevents the system from making assumptions about clinical nuance that could lead to incorrect coding if the provider's specific intent differs from standard clinical definitions.

section_header: 1. Locating a code in the ICD-10-CM
text:
To select a code in the classification that corresponds to a diagnosis or reason for visit documented in a medical record, first locate the term in the Alphabetic Index, and then verify the code in the Tabular List. Read and be guided by instructional notations that appear in both the Alphabetic Index and the Tabular List.

It is essential to use both the Alphabetic Index and Tabular List when locating and assigning a code. The Alphabetic Index does not always provide the full code. Selection of the full code, including laterality and any applicable 7 th character can only be done in the Tabular List. A dash (-) at the end of an Alphabetic Index entry indicates that additional characters are required. Even if a dash is not included at the Alphabetic Index entry, it is necessary to refer to the Tabular List to verify that no 7 th character is required.
description: Defines the process of locating and assigning diagnosis codes by using the Alphabetic Index for initial terms and the Tabular List for full code verification and instructional notations.

qna.q: Why is it necessary to verify a code in the Tabular List even when the Alphabetic Index entry lacks a dash indicating missing characters?
qna.a: The Alphabetic Index serves as a starting point but may omit specific requirements like 7th characters to keep the index concise. The Tabular List acts as the definitive authority where instructional notations explicitly state whether a character is mandatory, preventing the assignment of incomplete or invalid codes.

qna.q: How does the presence of a dash (-) in the Alphabetic Index change the workflow for selecting a diagnosis code?
qna.a: A dash signals that the entry is incomplete and requires further navigation to the Tabular List to append necessary characters like laterality or extension codes. This mechanism ensures that the final code accurately reflects the clinical details of the encounter, such as whether a fracture is open or closed.

qna.q: What are the real-world implications of failing to read instructional notations in both the Alphabetic Index and Tabular List?
qna.a: Ignoring these notations can lead to assigning a code that does not fully capture the severity or nature of the condition, resulting in claim denials or incorrect reimbursement. For example, missing a required 7th character might cause a hospital to be paid for a standard stay instead of a prolonged stay requiring specialized care.

text:
Diagnosis codes are to be used and reported at their highest number of characters available and to the highest level of specificity documented in the medical record.

ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes are composed of codes with 3, 4, 5, 6 or 7 characters. Codes with three characters are included in ICD-10-CM as the heading of a category of codes that may be further subdivided by the use of fourth and/or fifth characters and/or sixth characters, which provide greater detail.

A three-character code is to be used only if it is not further subdivided. A code is invalid if it has not been coded to the full number of characters required for that code, including the 7 th character, if applicable.
description: Mandates the use of diagnosis codes at their highest character count and level of specificity as documented in the medical record. Invalidates codes that are not fully expanded to the required number of characters, including the seventh character when applicable.

qna.q: Why does the system mandate using the highest number of characters available rather than accepting shorter, less specific codes?
qna.a: Shorter codes often represent broad categories that lack the clinical detail needed for accurate epidemiology, billing, and treatment tracking. Using the full specificity ensures that data reflects the true complexity of the patient's condition, which is critical for quality reporting and reimbursement accuracy.

qna.q: What happens if a code is submitted with fewer characters than required, such as missing the mandatory seventh character?
qna.a: The code is treated as invalid because the seventh character often distinguishes between different stages of a condition, like acute versus chronic. Submitting an incomplete code prevents the system from correctly categorizing the diagnosis, potentially leading to claim denials or loss of data integrity.

section_header: 3. Code or codes from A00.0 through T88.9, Z00-Z99.8, U00-U85
text: The appropriate code or codes from A00.0 through T88.9, Z00-Z99.8, and U00-U85 must be used to identify diagnoses, symptoms, conditions, problems, complaints or other reason(s) for the encounter/visit.
description: Specifies the required use of codes from ranges A00.0 through T88.9, Z00-Z99.8, and U00-U85 to identify diagnoses and symptoms for an encounter visit.

text:
Codes that describe symptoms and signs, as opposed to diagnoses, are acceptable for reporting purposes when a related definitive diagnosis has not been established (confirmed) by the provider. Chapter 18 of ICD-10-CM, Symptoms, Signs, and Abnormal Clinical and Laboratory Findings, Not Elsewhere Classified (codes R00.0 - R99) contains many, but not all, codes for symptoms.

See Section I.B.18. Use of Signs/Symptom/Unspecified Codes
master_label: Symptom and Sign Code Reporting Guidelines
description: Establishes that ICD-10-CM symptom and sign codes (R00.0-R99) are acceptable for reporting when a definitive diagnosis has not been confirmed by the provider.

qna.q: Why does the system allow symptom codes like R00.0-R99 to be reported when a definitive diagnosis hasn't been confirmed yet?
qna.a: This rule exists because patients often present with acute or ambiguous symptoms before a provider can identify the underlying cause, creating a gap where no specific diagnosis code exists. By permitting these codes, the system ensures that the clinical encounter is accurately captured for billing and tracking purposes without forcing a premature or speculative diagnosis.

section_header: 5. Conditions that are an integral part of a disease process
text: Signs and symptoms that are associated routinely with a disease process should not be assigned as additional codes, unless otherwise instructed by the classification.
master_label: ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines for Signs and Symptoms
description: Establishes that signs and symptoms routinely associated with a disease process must not be coded as additional diagnoses unless specific classification instructions dictate otherwise.

qna.q: Why does the classification system explicitly forbid coding routine signs and symptoms as additional codes?
qna.a: This restriction prevents 'code inflation' by ensuring that only the primary disease process drives the diagnosis, rather than listing every associated symptom that naturally occurs with it. By excluding these routine findings, the system maintains data integrity for epidemiological tracking and ensures that reimbursement reflects the actual complexity of the underlying condition rather than a list of expected clinical manifestations.

section_header: 6. Conditions that are not an integral part of a disease process
text: Additional signs and symptoms that may not be associated routinely with a disease process should be coded when present.
description: This passage implements the requirement to code additional signs and symptoms that are not routinely associated with a disease process when they are present in a patient record. It ensures that these specific clinical findings are captured as distinct codes rather than being omitted or subsumed under the primary diagnosis.

section_header: 7. Multiple coding for a single condition
text:
In addition to the etiology/manifestation convention that requires two codes to fully describe a single condition that affects multiple body systems, there are other single conditions that also require more than one code. 'Use additional code' notes are found in the Tabular List at codes that are not part of an etiology/manifestation pair where a secondary code is useful to fully describe a condition. The sequencing rule is the same as the etiology/manifestation pair, 'use additional code' indicates that a secondary code should be added , if known.

For example, for bacterial infections that are not included in chapter 1, a secondary code from category B95, Streptococcus, Staphylococcus, and Enterococcus, as the cause of diseases classified elsewhere, or B96, Other bacterial agents as the cause of diseases classified elsewhere, may be required to identify the bacterial organism causing the infection. A 'use additional code' note will normally be found at the infectious disease code, indicating a need for the organism code to be added as a secondary code.

'Code first' notes are also under certain codes that are not specifically manifestation codes but may be due to an underlying cause. When there is a 'code first' note and an underlying condition is present, the underlying condition should be sequenced first, if known.

'Code, if applicable, any causal condition first' notes indicate that this code may be assigned as a principal diagnosis when the causal condition is unknown or not applicable. If a causal condition is known, then the code for that condition should be sequenced as the principal or first-listed diagnosis.

Multiple codes may be needed for sequela, complication codes and obstetric codes to more fully describe a condition. See the specific guidelines for these conditions for further instruction.
description: Defines sequencing rules requiring multiple codes to fully describe single conditions affecting multiple body systems or bacterial infections. It mandates adding secondary codes from specific categories when 'use additional code' notes are present in the Tabular List.

qna.q: Why does the system require a secondary code from categories B95 or B96 for bacterial infections not in Chapter 1?
qna.a: This requirement exists because many infectious disease codes describe the clinical syndrome rather than the specific pathogen, which is critical for tracking antibiotic resistance and epidemiology. Without the secondary organism code, the data lacks the granularity needed to distinguish between, for example, a generic 'sepsis' case and one caused specifically by a multidrug-resistant Staphylococcus strain.

qna.q: How does the 'Code first' note mechanism alter the sequencing logic when an underlying condition is present?
qna.a: The mechanism forces the coding engine to prioritize the root cause over the manifestation, ensuring the principal diagnosis reflects the true origin of the patient's health issue. This prevents the clinical picture (the symptom) from masking the etiology, which is essential for accurate resource allocation and understanding disease progression.

qna.q: What are the real-world implications of using 'Code, if applicable, any causal condition first' when the cause is unknown?
qna.a: This rule allows the system to assign a principal diagnosis for the acute condition (like sepsis) without leaving the record incomplete, while still flagging that the etiology is currently unidentified. It ensures immediate clinical action can be taken for the patient's current state while maintaining data integrity regarding the uncertainty of the root cause.

text: If the same condition is described as both acute (subacute) and chronic, and separate subentries exist in the Alphabetic Index at the same indentation level, code both and sequence the acute (subacute) code first.
master_label: Acute and Chronic Condition Coding Rules
description: Establishes coding guidelines for conditions described as both acute and chronic when separate subentries exist at the same index indentation level. Requires sequencing the acute or subacute code first when both are assigned.

qna.q: Why does the guideline mandate sequencing the acute code before the chronic one when both are present?
qna.a: This sequencing reflects the clinical reality that acute conditions represent the immediate, active phase of a disease requiring urgent intervention, while chronic codes describe the long-term status. Placing the acute code first ensures the primary reason for the current encounter is captured as the principal diagnosis, which directly influences treatment planning and resource allocation.

text:
A combination code is a single code used to classify: Two diagnoses, or A diagnosis with an associated secondary process (manifestation) A diagnosis with an associated complication

Combination codes are identified by referring to subterm entries in the Alphabetic Index and by reading the inclusion and exclusion notes in the Tabular List.

Assign only the combination code when that code fully identifies the diagnostic conditions involved or when the Alphabetic Index so directs. Multiple coding should not be used when the classification provides a combination code that clearly identifies all of the elements documented in the diagnosis. When the combination code lacks necessary specificity in describing the manifestation or complication, an additional code should be used as a secondary code.
description: Defines the rules for assigning single combination codes that identify diagnoses with associated complications or manifestations. It specifies when to use a single code versus adding a secondary code based on the specificity of the classification.

qna.q: Why does the guideline explicitly forbid using multiple codes when a single combination code fully identifies the conditions?
qna.a: This restriction prevents 'code stacking' that would artificially inflate reimbursement or complicate clinical summaries by listing redundant information. The system relies on the combination code to capture the entire clinical picture in one entry, ensuring data consistency and reducing administrative burden.

qna.q: How does the mechanism determine when a combination code lacks the necessary specificity to stand alone?
qna.a: The mechanism relies on the coder to cross-reference inclusion and exclusion notes in the Tabular List to verify if the code covers all documented nuances. If the code fails to distinguish between the primary diagnosis and the specific manifestation or complication, the system requires an additional secondary code to fill that gap.

qna.q: What are the real-world implications for a coder who ignores the instruction to use a combination code when the Index directs it?
qna.a: Failing to use the directed combination code risks claim denials or audits because the submitted data no longer matches the standardized classification structure. It also creates ambiguity in patient records, making it difficult to track the relationship between a disease and its associated complications or processes.

text:
A sequela is the residual effect (condition produced) after the acute phase of an illness or injury has terminated. There is no time limit on when a sequela code can be used. The residual may be apparent early, such as in cerebral infarction, or it may occur months or years later, such as that due to a previous injury. Examples of sequela include: scar formation resulting from a burn, deviated septum due to a nasal fracture, and infertility due to tubal occlusion from old tuberculosis. Coding of sequela generally requires two codes sequenced in the following order: the condition or nature of the sequela is sequenced first. The sequela code is sequenced second.

An exception to the above guidelines are those instances where the code for the sequela is followed by a manifestation code identified in the Tabular List and title, or the sequela code has been expanded (at the fourth, fifth or sixth character levels) to include the manifestation(s). The code for the acute phase of an illness or injury that led to the sequela is never used with a code for the late effect.

See Section I.C.9. Sequelae of cerebrovascular disease

See Section I.C.15. Sequelae of complication of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium

See Section I.C.19. Application of 7 th characters for Chapter 19
master_label: Sequela and Late Effect Coding Guidelines
description: Defines the coding rules for residual conditions following the termination of an acute illness or injury, requiring the sequela nature to be sequenced first. It clarifies that there is no time limit for assigning these codes and provides examples such as scar formation or deviated septum.

qna.q: Why does the guideline explicitly state that the acute phase code is never used with a sequela code?
qna.a: This rule prevents double-counting the same disease episode by ensuring the acute event and its long-term residual effect are coded as a single clinical narrative. Using both would artificially inflate case counts and obscure the true timeline of the patient's condition, which is critical for accurate epidemiological tracking.

qna.q: How does the requirement to sequence the sequela code second impact the logic of ICD-10 coding algorithms?
qna.a: The sequencing order forces the coding engine to prioritize the current, observable condition over the historical cause when determining the primary diagnosis. This ensures that treatment planning and resource allocation focus on managing the active residual effects rather than the resolved acute illness.

qna.q: What does the lack of a time limit on sequela codes imply for data consistency in long-term health records?
qna.a: It implies that the coding system must remain flexible enough to capture conditions that manifest years after the initial injury, such as infertility from old tuberculosis. This flexibility ensures that historical data remains clinically relevant for decades, allowing for accurate long-term outcome studies.

text:
Code any condition described at the time of discharge as 'impending' or 'threatened' as follows:

If it did occur, code as confirmed diagnosis.

If it did not occur, reference the Alphabetic Index to determine if the condition has a subentry term for 'impending' or 'threatened' and also reference main term entries for 'Impending' and for 'Threatened.'

If the subterms are listed, assign the given code.

If the subterms are not listed, code the existing underlying condition(s) and not the condition described as impending or threatened.
description: Defines the coding logic for conditions described as impending or threatened at discharge, requiring verification via the Alphabetic Index or assignment of underlying condition codes if subterms are absent.

qna.q: Why does the guideline require coding the underlying condition instead of the impending one when no specific subterm exists?
qna.a: This rule prevents the artificial inflation of morbidity statistics by ensuring that only clinically realized diagnoses are counted as confirmed conditions. Without this constraint, providers might code 'impending' states as actual events to meet reporting thresholds, which would distort epidemiological data and resource allocation models.

qna.q: How does the instruction to check the Alphabetic Index for subentries affect the coding workflow for conditions like 'impending heart failure'?
qna.a: The coder must first verify if a specific code exists for the threatened state; if the index lacks a subterm, the workflow shifts to coding the root cause (e.g., heart failure) rather than the potential outcome. This ensures the medical record reflects the actual physiological pathology driving the patient's status at discharge rather than a speculative future event.

section_header: 12. Reporting Same Diagnosis Code More than Once
text: Each unique ICD-10-CM diagnosis code may be reported only once for an encounter. This applies to bilateral conditions when there are no distinct codes identifying laterality or two different conditions classified to the same ICD-10-CM diagnosis code.
description: Enforces a rule limiting each unique ICD-10-CM diagnosis code to a single report per encounter, even for bilateral conditions lacking distinct laterality codes.

qna.q: Why does the system enforce a single report per unique ICD-10-CM code even for bilateral conditions, and what does this imply for coding accuracy?
qna.a: This rule prevents data redundancy and ensures that the specific code selected captures the clinical essence of the encounter without inflating the count of distinct diagnoses. It implies that coders must choose the most precise code that inherently covers laterality or select a single representative code, rather than listing the same code twice for left and right sides.

text:
Some ICD-10-CM codes indicate laterality, specifying whether the condition occurs on the left, right or is bilateral. If no bilateral code is provided and the condition is bilateral, assign separate codes for both the left and right side. If the side is not identified in the medical record, assign the code for the unspecified side.

When a patient has a bilateral condition and each side is treated during separate encounters, assign the "bilateral" code (as the condition still exists on both sides), including for the encounter to treat the first side. For the second encounter for treatment after one side has previously been treated and the condition no longer exists on that side, assign the appropriate unilateral code for the side where the condition still exists (e.g., cataract surgery performed on each eye in separate encounters). The bilateral code would not be assigned for the subsequent encounter, as the patient no longer has the condition in the previously-treated site. If the treatment on the first side did not completely resolve the condition, then the bilateral code would still be appropriate.

When laterality is not documented by the patient's provider, code assignment for the affected side may be based on medical record documentation from other clinicians. If there is conflicting medical record documentation regarding the affected side, the patient's provider should be queried for clarification. Codes for 'unspecified' side should rarely be used, such as when the documentation in the record is insufficient to determine the affected side and it is not possible to obtain clarification.
description: Establishes coding rules for ICD-10-CM conditions based on laterality, specifying when to assign bilateral codes versus separate unilateral codes for left and right sides.

qna.q: Why does the rule require assigning separate unilateral codes for both sides when a bilateral condition is documented but no specific bilateral code exists?
qna.a: This approach ensures that the medical record accurately reflects the anatomical reality of the condition without forcing a specific laterality that wasn't clinically observed. Assigning separate codes prevents the artificial inflation of bilateral statistics and allows for more granular tracking of left versus right side prevalence in the dataset.

qna.q: How does the instruction to use the 'bilateral' code during the first encounter of a two-sided treatment affect subsequent billing or coding logic?
qna.a: Using the bilateral code initially acknowledges that the underlying pathology persists on both sides, even if only one is being treated at that moment. This distinction is critical for the second encounter, where the coder must switch to a unilateral code once the condition resolves on the first side, preventing the inappropriate re-application of a bilateral modifier for a resolved site.

qna.q: What are the real-world implications of relying on documentation from other clinicians when the patient's provider fails to specify the affected side?
qna.a: This mechanism acts as a safety net to prevent the overuse of 'unspecified' codes, which can obscure epidemiological trends and complicate quality reporting. However, it also introduces a dependency on the consistency of documentation across different care settings, potentially leading to coding discrepancies if the secondary records contradict the primary provider's notes.

section_header: 14. Documentation by Clinicians Other than the Patient's Provider
text:
Code assignment is based on the documentation by the patient's provider (i.e., physician or other qualified healthcare practitioner legally accountable for establishing the patient's diagnosis). There are a few exceptions when code assignment may be based on medical record documentation from clinicians who are not the patient's provider (i.e., physician or other qualified healthcare practitioner legally accountable for establishing the patient's diagnosis). In this context, 'clinicians' other than the patient's provider refer to healthcare professionals permitted, based on regulatory or accreditation requirements or internal hospital policies, to document in a patient's official medical record.

These exceptions include codes for:

- Body Mass Index (BMI)
- Depth of non-pressure chronic ulcers
- Pressure ulcer stage
- Coma scale
- NIH stroke scale (NIHSS)
- Social determinants of health (SDOH) classified to Chapter 21
- Laterality
- Blood alcohol level
- Underimmunization status

This information is typically , or may be, documented by other clinicians involved in the care of the patient (e.g., a dietitian often documents the BMI, a nurse often documents the pressure ulcer stages, and an emergency medical technician often documents the coma scale). However, the associated diagnosis (such as overweight, obesity, acute stroke, pressure ulcer, or a condition classifiable to category F10, Alcohol related disorders) must be documented by the patient's provider. If there is conflicting medical record documentation, either from the same clinician or different clinicians, the patient's provider should be queried for clarification.

The BMI, coma scale, NIHSS, blood alcohol level codes, codes for social determinants of health and underimmunization status should only be reported as secondary diagnoses.

See Section I.C.21.c.17. for additional information regarding coding social determinants of health.
master_label: Code Assignment and Diagnosis Exception Logic
description: Defines code assignment based on the patient's provider's documentation while allowing specific exceptions for BMI and chronic ulcer depth codes documented by other authorized clinicians.

qna.q: Why is the patient's provider required to document the primary diagnosis even when other clinicians record the specific metrics like BMI or coma scales?
qna.a: This requirement exists because the provider holds the legal accountability for establishing the patient's overall diagnosis, whereas other clinicians only document specific clinical observations. Without the provider's explicit diagnosis, the system cannot legally link the metric (e.g., a BMI number) to a billable condition like obesity.

qna.q: How does the rule that these specific codes must be reported as secondary diagnoses impact the structure of a medical record?
qna.a: This mechanism prevents these metrics from being treated as the primary reason for a hospital admission or encounter, ensuring they are coded as contributing factors rather than the main event. It forces the coding hierarchy to prioritize the provider's established diagnosis over the clinical measurements taken by nurses or technicians.

qna.q: What are the real-world implications for billing when conflicting documentation exists between the provider and other clinicians regarding these exceptions?
qna.a: In cases of conflict, the system mandates querying the provider for clarification because their documentation overrides all other clinical notes for the primary diagnosis. This creates a workflow bottleneck where billing cannot be finalized until the legally accountable provider resolves the discrepancy.

text: Follow the Alphabetic Index guidance when coding syndromes. In the absence of Alphabetic Index guidance, assign codes for the documented manifestations of the syndrome. Additional codes for manifestations that are not an integral part of the disease process may also be assigned when the condition does not have a unique code.
description: Provides instructions to follow Alphabetic Index guidance when coding syndromes, assigning codes for documented manifestations if specific guidance is absent.

qna.q: Why does the guideline prioritize assigning codes for documented manifestations over the syndrome itself when Alphabetic Index guidance is absent?
qna.a: This approach ensures clinical accuracy by capturing the specific, observable pathology rather than relying on a potentially vague syndromic label. It prevents under-coding where a unique manifestation code exists but the overarching syndrome lacks a specific entry, ensuring the medical record reflects the actual treatment provided.

text: Code assignment is based on the provider's documentation of the relationship between the condition and the care or procedure , unless otherwise instructed by the classification. The guideline extends to any complications of care, regardless of the chapter the code is located in. It is important to note that not all conditions that occur during or following medical care or surgery are classified as complications. There must be a cause-and-effect relationship between the care provided and the condition, and the documentation must support that the condition is clinically significant. It is not necessary for the provider to explicitly document the term 'complication.' For example, if the condition alters the course of the surgery as documented in the operative report, then it would be appropriate to report a complication code. Query the provider for clarification if the documentation is not clear as to the relationship between the condition and the care or procedure.
description: Defines code assignment rules requiring a cause-and-effect relationship between care and conditions to classify clinically significant complications. The guidelines extend to any complications regardless of chapter, relying on provider documentation that the condition alters the course of surgery or care.

qna.q: Why is the explicit term 'complication' not required for code assignment if the condition alters the surgical course?
qna.a: The system relies on clinical significance and cause-and-effect relationships rather than specific terminology to ensure accurate coding. If the operative report shows the condition changed the procedure, it implies a direct link to the care provided, making the label unnecessary for proper classification.

qna.q: How does the requirement for a cause-and-effect relationship prevent misclassification of incidental conditions?
qna.a: This mechanism filters out conditions that merely happen to occur during care but are unrelated to the treatment itself. By demanding documented evidence that the care caused the condition, the guideline ensures only clinically significant complications are coded, reducing false positives.

qna.q: What are the real-world implications when documentation is unclear about the relationship between a condition and the procedure?
qna.a: In such cases, the workflow mandates querying the provider for clarification before assigning a code to avoid billing errors. This step ensures that the final code reflects the true clinical picture rather than assumptions made from ambiguous notes.

text: If the provider documents a "borderline" diagnosis at the time of discharge, the diagnosis is coded as confirmed, unless the classification provides a specific entry (e.g., borderline diabetes). If a borderline condition has a specific index entry in ICD-10-CM, it should be coded as such. Since borderline conditions are not uncertain diagnoses, no distinction is made between the care setting (inpatient versus outpatient). Whenever the documentation is unclear regarding a borderline condition, coders are encouraged to query for clarification.
description: Establishes coding rules for borderline diagnoses at discharge, requiring specific ICD-10-CM index entries and eliminating distinctions between inpatient and outpatient settings. Coders are instructed to query for clarification when documentation regarding borderline conditions is unclear.

qna.q: Why does the system treat a documented 'borderline' diagnosis as confirmed rather than uncertain, and what are the implications for coding accuracy?
qna.a: This change reflects the clinical reality that 'borderline' implies a definitive threshold has been met, distinguishing it from 'uncertain' where the provider is unsure of the presence of the condition. Consequently, coders should not apply uncertainty modifiers or differentiate between inpatient and outpatient settings, but instead code the specific borderline entry if available or query the provider if the documentation remains ambiguous.

text:
Sign/symptom and 'unspecified' codes have acceptable, even necessary, uses. While specific diagnosis codes should be reported when they are supported by the available medical record documentation and clinical knowledge of the patient's health condition, there are instances when signs/symptoms or unspecified codes are the best choices for accurately reflecting the healthcare encounter. Each healthcare encounter should be coded to the level of certainty known for that encounter.

As stated in the introductory section of these official coding guidelines, a joint effort between the healthcare provider and the coder is essential to achieve complete and accurate documentation, code assignment, and reporting of diagnoses and procedures. The importance of consistent, complete documentation in the medical record cannot be overemphasized. Without such documentation accurate coding cannot be achieved. The entire record should be reviewed to determine the specific reason for the encounter and the conditions treated.

If a definitive diagnosis has not been established by the end of the encounter, it is appropriate to report codes for sign(s) and/or symptom(s) in lieu of a definitive diagnosis. When sufficient clinical information isn't known or available about a particular health condition to assign a more specific code, it is acceptable to report the appropriate 'unspecified' code (e.g., a diagnosis of pneumonia has been determined, but not the specific type). Unspecified codes should be reported when they are the codes that most accurately reflect what is known about the patient's condition at the time of that particular encounter. It would be inappropriate to select a specific code that is not supported by the medical record documentation or conduct medically unnecessary diagnostic testing in order to determine a more specific code.
description: Establishes guidelines for using sign/symptom and unspecified diagnosis codes when specific codes are not supported by medical documentation. Emphasizes the need for accurate coding based on the level of certainty known during the healthcare encounter.

qna.q: Why is it appropriate to use sign/symptom codes when a definitive diagnosis hasn't been established by the end of an encounter?
qna.a: This approach ensures the coded data accurately reflects the level of certainty known at the time of the visit rather than guessing a specific condition. It prevents the medical record from implying a confirmed diagnosis that the clinician has not yet verified, maintaining the integrity of the clinical timeline.

qna.q: How does the requirement for 'complete documentation' directly impact the selection between specific and unspecified codes?
qna.a: The mechanism relies on the medical record serving as the sole evidence for code assignment; without sufficient clinical details to support a specific code, the system defaults to an unspecified category. This prevents coders from selecting a more specific code based on assumptions or external knowledge rather than the actual documented facts.

qna.q: What are the real-world implications of prohibiting medically unnecessary diagnostic testing just to achieve a more specific code?
qna.a: This rule acts as a financial and ethical safeguard, ensuring that healthcare providers do not order extra tests solely to satisfy coding granularity requirements. It aligns reimbursement with the actual scope of care provided, preventing upcoding and reducing patient exposure to unnecessary procedures.

section_header: a. Use of External Cause of Morbidity Codes
text:
An external cause of morbidity code should be assigned to identify the cause of the injury(ies) incurred as a result of the hurricane. The use of external cause of morbidity codes is supplemental to the application of ICD-10-CM codes. External cause of morbidity codes are never to be recorded as a principal diagnosis (firstlisted in non-inpatient settings). The appropriate injury code should be sequenced before any external cause codes. The external cause of morbidity codes capture how the injury or health condition happened (cause), the intent (unintentional or accidental; or intentional, such as suicide or assault), the place where the event occurred, the activity of the patient at the time of the event, and the person's status

(e.g., civilian, military). They should not be assigned for encounters to treat hurricane victims' medical conditions when no injury, adverse effect or poisoning is involved. External cause of morbidity codes should be assigned for each encounter for care and treatment of the injury. External cause of morbidity codes may be assigned in all health care settings. For the purpose of capturing complete and accurate ICD-10-CM data in the aftermath of the hurricane, a healthcare setting should be considered as any location where medical care is provided by licensed healthcare professionals.
description: Assigns external cause of morbidity codes to identify injuries resulting from the hurricane as a supplement to ICD-10-CM codes. These codes capture the cause, intent, location, and circumstances of the event but are never recorded as a principal diagnosis.

qna.q: Why must external cause codes be sequenced after the injury code rather than serving as the principal diagnosis?
qna.a: The injury code describes the specific pathology (the 'what'), while the external cause code describes the circumstances (the 'how' and 'why'). Placing the injury first ensures the primary clinical condition is captured for treatment, while the external cause acts as a necessary supplement for epidemiological tracking without overshadowing the immediate medical need.

qna.q: How does the requirement to assign these codes for every encounter impact data quality during mass casualty events like a hurricane?
qna.a: Mandating assignment for every encounter ensures that the 'who, where, and how' of the injury is consistently recorded regardless of the setting, preventing gaps in data that could skew disaster response analysis. This consistency allows public health officials to accurately map injury patterns to specific environmental hazards or activities, which is critical for resource allocation and prevention strategies.

qna.q: What does the exclusion of external cause codes for encounters treating non-injury medical conditions imply about the scope of hurricane-related care?
qna.a: This restriction implies that the coding system is designed specifically to track trauma and acute physical harm caused by the disaster, rather than general medical needs like dehydration or anxiety. By limiting the codes to injuries, adverse effects, or poisonings, the data remains focused on the direct physical consequences of the event, avoiding noise from unrelated health issues.

section_header: b. Sequencing of External Causes of Morbidity Codes
text: Codes for cataclysmic events, such as a hurricane, take priority over all other external cause codes except child and adult abuse and terrorism and should be sequenced before other external cause of injury codes. Assign as many external cause of morbidity codes as necessary to fully explain each cause. For example, if an injury occurs as a result of a building collapse during the hurricane, external cause codes for both the hurricane and the building collapse should be assigned, with the external causes code for hurricane being sequenced as the first external cause code. For injuries incurred as a direct result of the hurricane, assign the appropriate code(s) for the injuries, followed by the code X37.0-, Hurricane (with the appropriate 7th character), and any other applicable external cause of injury codes. Code X37.0- also should be assigned when an injury is incurred as a result of flooding caused by a levee breaking related to the hurricane. Code X38.-, Flood (with the appropriate 7th character), should be assigned when an injury is from flooding resulting directly from the storm. Code X36.0.-, Collapse of dam or manmade structure, should not be assigned when the cause of the collapse is due to the hurricane. Use of code X36.0- is limited to collapses of man-made structures due to earth surface movements, not due to storm surges directly from a hurricane.
description: Establishes priority sequencing rules for external cause codes related to cataclysmic events like hurricanes, ensuring they precede other injury codes except for abuse and terrorism. The update mandates assigning multiple codes to fully explain complex causes, such as building collapses during a hurricane, and specifies the use of code X37.0- for direct hurricane injuries.

qna.q: Why does the coding guideline mandate that hurricane codes take priority over other external cause codes, and what is the specific exception?
qna.a: This hierarchy ensures that the root environmental cause (the hurricane) is identified as the primary driver of the injury, which is critical for public health tracking and disaster response analysis. The exceptions for child/adult abuse and terrorism exist because those events represent distinct, non-environmental causal mechanisms that require separate investigative and legal frameworks.

qna.q: How does the instruction to assign multiple codes for a building collapse during a hurricane change the data interpretation compared to a standard collapse?
qna.a: By requiring both the hurricane code and the collapse code, the system captures the full causal chain rather than just the immediate mechanism of injury. This distinction prevents misclassifying storm-related structural failures as generic earth surface movements, ensuring accurate attribution of liability and resource allocation during natural disasters.

qna.q: What is the practical implication of the restriction preventing the use of code X36.0- for hurricane-induced dam collapses?
qna.a: This restriction forces coders to select the specific hurricane code (X37.0-) instead of the generic collapse code, ensuring that statistics on infrastructure failure are correctly attributed to weather events rather than geological ones. This accuracy is vital for policymakers assessing the true impact of climate-related disasters on civil infrastructure.

section_header: c. Other External Causes of Morbidity Code Issues
text:
For injuries that are not a direct result of the hurricane, such as an evacuee that has incurred an injury as a result of a motor vehicle accident, assign the appropriate external cause of morbidity code(s) to describe the cause of the injury, but do not assign code X37.0-, Hurricane. If it is not clear whether the injury was a direct result of the hurricane, assume the injury is due to the hurricane and assign code X37.0-, Hurricane, as well as any other applicable external cause of morbidity codes. In addition to code X37.0-, Hurricane, other possible applicable external cause of morbidity codes include:

X30-, Exposure to excessive natural heat

X31-, Exposure to excessive natural cold

X38-, Flood
description: Establishes coding rules for assigning external cause of morbidity codes to injuries sustained during hurricane evacuations, distinguishing between direct hurricane effects and unrelated accidents like motor vehicle incidents.

qna.q: Why does the guideline instruct coders to assume an injury is hurricane-related if the cause is unclear, rather than defaulting to a generic external cause?
qna.a: This default assumption prevents undercounting the hurricane's true impact on public health by ensuring all ambiguous cases are captured under the specific disaster code. Without this rule, coders might inadvertently classify uncertain injuries as unrelated accidents, skewing epidemiological data used for resource allocation and disaster response planning.

qna.q: How does the requirement to assign both the specific hurricane code (X37.0-) and applicable external cause codes (like X38- for flood) improve the utility of the data?
qna.a: Assigning both codes allows analysts to distinguish between the broad disaster event and the specific mechanism of injury, such as distinguishing a flood victim from someone injured by excessive heat. This granularity supports targeted interventions, ensuring that response teams can address specific hazards like floodwaters or heat exposure rather than treating all hurricane injuries as a single monolithic category.

text:
Z codes (other reasons for healthcare encounters) may be assigned as appropriate to further explain the reasons for presenting for healthcare services, including transfers between healthcare facilities, or provide additional information relevant to a patient encounter.
