This implies that coders must wait for final confirmation rather than relying on initial assumptions, ensuring the medical record reflects the actual reason for the surgery and the patient's true condition.

section_header: P. Encounters for general medical examinations with abnormal findings
text: The subcategories for encounters for general medical examinations, Z00.0- and encounter for routine child health examination, Z00.12-, provide codes for with and without abnormal findings. Should a general medical examination result in an abnormal finding, the code for general medical examination with abnormal finding should be assigned as the first-listed diagnosis. An examination with abnormal findings refers to a condition/diagnosis that is newly identified or a change in severity of a chronic condition (such as uncontrolled hypertension, or an acute exacerbation of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease) during a routine physical examination. A secondary code for the abnormal finding should also be coded.
description: Defines coding rules for general medical examinations (Z00) where abnormal findings, such as uncontrolled hypertension, are newly identified or represent a change in severity. It mandates assigning the examination with abnormal findings as the first-listed diagnosis and requires a secondary code for the specific condition.

qna.q: Why does the guideline mandate that an abnormal finding discovered during a routine exam becomes the first-listed diagnosis instead of the exam code?
qna.a: This prioritization ensures the primary reason for the encounter is the actual clinical issue found, rather than the administrative act of checking. By listing the abnormal condition first, the system accurately reflects that the visit's purpose shifted from a general check-up to treating a specific new or worsening health problem.

qna.q: How does reordering these codes impact the financial and clinical interpretation of a routine physical exam encounter?
qna.a: Clinically, it signals that the patient requires immediate intervention for the new condition rather than just observation. Financially, it ensures reimbursement aligns with the complexity of managing the acute or worsening issue, preventing underpayment that would occur if the low-complexity exam code were listed first.

section_header: Q. Encounters for routine health screenings
text: See Section I.C.21. Factors influencing health status and contact with health services, Screening
description: This passage implements the ICD-10-CM coding guidelines for assigning codes from Chapter 21 to document factors influencing health status and contact with health services, specifically for screening encounters. It ensures that codes for screening results are applied correctly when no diagnosis is confirmed, distinguishing between normal findings and abnormal results requiring further investigation.

text:
These guidelines are to be used as a supplement to the ICD-10-CM Official Guidelines for Coding and Reporting to facilitate the assignment of the Present on Admission (POA) indicator for each diagnosis and external cause of injury code reported on claim forms (UB-04 and 837 Institutional).

These guidelines are not intended to replace any guidelines in the main body of the ICD-10-CM Official Guidelines for Coding and Reporting . The POA guidelines are not intended to provide guidance on when a condition should be coded, but rather, how to apply the POA indicator to the final set of diagnosis codes that have been assigned in accordance with Sections I, II, and III of the official coding guidelines. Subsequent to the assignment of the ICD-10-CM codes, the POA indicator should then be assigned to those conditions that have been coded.

As stated in the Introduction to the ICD-10-CM Official Guidelines for Coding and Reporting, a joint effort between the healthcare provider and the coder is essential to achieve complete and accurate documentation, code assignment, and reporting of diagnoses and procedures. The importance of consistent, complete documentation in the medical record cannot be overemphasized. Medical record documentation from any provider involved in the care and treatment of the patient may be used to support the determination of whether a condition was present on admission or not. In the context of the official coding guidelines, the term 'provider' means a physician or any qualified healthcare practitioner who is legally accountable for establishing the patient's diagnosis.

These guidelines are not a substitute for the provider's clinical judgment as to the determination of whether a condition was/was not present on admission. The provider should be queried regarding issues related to the linking of signs/symptoms, timing of test results, and the timing of findings.

Please see the CDC website for the detailed list of ICD-10-CM codes that do not require the use of a POA indicator (https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/icd/icd-10-cm/files.html) . The codes and categories on this exempt list are for circumstances regarding the healthcare encounter or factors influencing health status that do not represent a current disease or injury or that describe conditions that are always present on admission.
description: Supplements ICD-10-CM Official Guidelines to facilitate the assignment of the Present on Admission (POA) indicator for diagnoses and injury codes on institutional claim forms.

qna.q: Why is the assignment of the POA indicator described as a supplement to the main ICD-10-CM guidelines rather than a standalone rule set?
qna.a: The POA indicator is a reporting attribute applied only after the primary diagnosis codes are established, meaning it relies entirely on the clinical validity of the initial code assignment. Without the foundational logic in Sections I, II, and III to determine what conditions exist, there is no valid condition to which the 'present on admission' flag can logically be attached.

qna.q: How does the requirement for a 'joint effort' between the provider and coder actually change the workflow for determining if a condition was present on admission?
qna.a: This mechanism shifts the burden of proof from the coder guessing based on notes to a collaborative verification where the provider must explicitly confirm the timing of findings. It forces a specific query loop where the provider clarifies the link between symptoms and test results, ensuring the medical record supports the claim that the condition existed prior to hospital admission.

qna.q: What are the real-world implications for healthcare providers if they fail to document the timing of findings clearly enough for the coder to make a POA determination?
qna.a: Ambiguous documentation prevents the coder from applying the correct indicator, which can lead to the condition being incorrectly flagged as 'not present on admission.' This misclassification directly impacts hospital reimbursement rates and can trigger compliance penalties, as the financial value of the claim hinges on this specific binary flag.

text: All claims involving inpatient admissions to general acute care hospitals or other facilities that are subject to a law or regulation mandating collection of present on admission information.
master_label: Present on Admission Data Collection Mandate
description: Defines the scope of claims requiring present on admission information for inpatient admissions at general acute care facilities subject to collection mandates.

qna.q: Why does the reporting requirement specifically target inpatient admissions to general acute care hospitals rather than all healthcare facilities?
qna.a: This distinction exists because present-on-admission (POA) indicators are legally mandated primarily for acute care settings where the clinical distinction between pre-existing conditions and new complications directly impacts hospital reimbursement rates. By limiting the scope to these facilities, the regulation ensures that payers only process data where the POA flag has a direct financial and clinical consequence, avoiding unnecessary administrative burden on outpatient or specialized care providers.

text:
Present on admission is defined as present at the time the order for inpatient admission occurs -- conditions that develop during an outpatient encounter, including emergency department, observation, or outpatient surgery, are considered as present on admission.

POA indicator is assigned to principal and secondary diagnoses (as defined in Section II of the Official Guidelines for Coding and Reporting) and the external cause of injury codes.

Issues related to inconsistent, missing, conflicting or unclear documentation must still be resolved by the provider.

If a condition would not be coded and reported based on UHDDS definitions and current official coding guidelines, then the POA indicator would not be reported.
description: Defines the Present on Admission status based on the timing of inpatient orders versus outpatient encounters and assigns the POA indicator to principal, secondary, and external cause of injury codes.

qna.q: Why does the definition explicitly include conditions developing during outpatient encounters like emergency department visits as 'present on admission'?
qna.a: This clarification prevents coding errors where providers might mistakenly mark a condition as 'not present on admission' simply because it was first identified in an outpatient setting. The rule ensures that if a patient has a condition before the inpatient order is placed, even if it worsens later, it must be flagged as POA to accurately reflect the timeline of care.

qna.q: What are the real-world implications for providers when the text states that inconsistent or missing documentation must still be resolved by them?
qna.a: This implies that automated systems cannot automatically assign the POA indicator if the clinical picture is ambiguous; the burden of proof remains on the provider to clarify the timeline. Failure to resolve these discrepancies can lead to claim denials or penalties, as the indicator is critical for determining hospital liability and reimbursement rates.

text:
Y - Yes

N - No

U - Unknown

W - Clinically undetermined

Unreported/Not used - (Exempt from POA reporting)
description: This logic implements the classification of "Unreported/Not used" status as an exemption from Point of Admission (POA) reporting requirements. It ensures that conditions marked with this specific value are excluded from mandatory POA data submission workflows.

qna.q: What is the operational implication of the 'W' (Clinically undetermined) status compared to 'U' (Unknown) in POA reporting?
qna.a: The 'W' status indicates that clinical evidence exists but is insufficient to definitively classify the condition as present on admission, whereas 'U' suggests a complete lack of data. This distinction allows hospitals to flag cases for further review rather than automatically excluding them or counting them as acquired, ensuring more accurate quality metrics while preventing premature penalties for ambiguous cases.

text:
Y = present at the time of inpatient admission

N = not present at the time of inpatient admission

U = documentation is insufficient to determine if condition is present on admission

W = provider is unable to clinically determine whether condition was present on admission or not
description: Defines four status codes (Y, N, U, W) to classify whether a condition was present at the time of inpatient admission based on clinical documentation and provider assessment.

qna.q: What are the real-world implications of using 'U' (documentation insufficient) versus 'W' (clinically undeterminable) for Present on Admission status?
qna.a: The distinction matters because 'U' indicates a documentation gap that should be corrected to ensure accurate billing and compliance, whereas 'W' reflects a genuine clinical uncertainty where the provider cannot determine the status despite best efforts. Using 'U' for a clinically unknown condition could incorrectly trigger denial of coverage or misrepresent the patient's condition at admission.

section_header: Timeframe for POA Identification and Documentation
text:
There is no required timeframe as to when a provider (per the definition of 'provider' used in these guidelines) must identify or document a condition to be present on admission. In some clinical situations, it may not be possible for a provider to make a definitive diagnosis (or a condition may not be recognized or reported by the patient) for a period of time after admission. In some cases, it may be several days before the provider arrives at a definitive diagnosis. This does not mean that the condition was not present on admission. Determination of whether the condition was present on admission or not will be based on the applicable POA guideline as identified in this document, or on the provider's best clinical judgment.

If at the time of code assignment the documentation is unclear as to whether a condition was present on admission or not, it is appropriate to query the provider for clarification.
description: Clarifies that there is no mandatory timeframe for providers to document a condition to be present on admission, allowing for definitive diagnoses to be established days after admission without affecting POA status.

qna.q: Why does the guideline explicitly state there is no required timeframe for documenting a Present on Admission (POA) condition?
qna.a: This flexibility exists because clinical reality often involves a lag between admission and the provider's ability to make a definitive diagnosis. Waiting for a specific deadline could force providers to guess or miscode conditions that are actually pre-existing but undetected at the moment of admission.

qna.q: How does the mechanism of 'provider best clinical judgment' resolve cases where documentation remains unclear after the initial code assignment?
qna.a: The system relies on a query mechanism where coders ask the provider for clarification rather than defaulting to a specific status. This ensures the final coding decision reflects the actual clinical timeline of the illness rather than the administrative timing of the note.

qna.q: What are the real-world implications of allowing several days to pass before a definitive diagnosis is made?
qna.a: This prevents the automatic misclassification of pre-existing conditions as acute complications simply because the diagnosis was delayed. It protects payers from denying claims for conditions that were indeed present on admission, even if the medical record initially lacked the specific label.

text:
- Condition is on the 'Exempt from Reporting' list

Leave the 'present on admission' field blank if the condition is on the list of ICD10-CM codes for which this field is not applicable. This is the only circumstance in which the field may be left blank.

- POA Explicitly Documented Assign Y for any condition the provider explicitly documents as being present on admission.

Assign N for any condition the provider explicitly documents as not present at the time of admission.

- Conditions diagnosed prior to inpatient admission Assign 'Y' for conditions that were diagnosed prior to admission (example:

hypertension, diabetes mellitus, asthma)

- Conditions diagnosed during the admission but clearly present before admission Assign 'Y' for conditions diagnosed during the admission that were clearly present but not diagnosed until after admission occurred.

Diagnoses subsequently confirmed after admission are considered present on admission if at the time of admission they are documented as suspected, possible, rule out, differential diagnosis, or constitute an underlying cause of a symptom that is present at the time of admission.

- Condition develops during outpatient encounter prior to inpatient admission Assign Y for any condition that develops during an outpatient encounter prior to a written order for inpatient admission.
- Documentation does not indicate whether condition was present on admission Assign 'U' when the medical record documentation is unclear as to whether the condition was present on admission. 'U' should not be routinely assigned and used only in very limited circumstances. Coders are encouraged to query the providers when the documentation is unclear.

Documentation states that it cannot be determined whether the condition was or was not present on admission

- Assign 'W' when the medical record documentation indicates that it cannot be

clinically determined whether or not the condition was present on admission.

- Chronic condition with acute exacerbation during the admission If a single code identifies both the chronic condition and the acute exacerbation, see POA guidelines pertaining to codes that contain multiple clinical concepts.

If a single code only identifies the chronic condition and not the acute exacerbation (e.g., acute exacerbation of chronic leukemia), assign 'Y.'

Conditions documented as possible, probable, suspected, or rule out at the time of discharge

If the final diagnosis contains a possible, probable, suspected, or rule out diagnosis, and this diagnosis was based on signs, symptoms or clinical findings suspected at the time of inpatient admission, assign 'Y.'

If the final diagnosis contains a possible, probable, suspected, or rule out diagnosis, and this diagnosis was based on signs, symptoms or clinical findings that were not present on admission, assign 'N'.

- Conditions documented as impending or threatened at the time of discharge If the final diagnosis contains an impending or threatened diagnosis, and this diagnosis is based on symptoms or clinical findings that were present on admission, assign 'Y'.

If the final diagnosis contains an impending or threatened diagnosis, and this diagnosis is based on symptoms or clinical findings that were not present on admission, assign 'N'.
description: This logic implements the ICD-10 POA reporting rules by leaving the field blank for exempt codes, assigning 'Y' for conditions explicitly documented as present on admission or diagnosed prior to admission, and assigning 'N' for those explicitly documented as not present at admission.

qna.q: Why is the 'U' (Uncertain) flag restricted to very limited circumstances compared to the 'W' (Clinically Undetermined) flag?
qna.a: The distinction exists because 'U' implies a documentation gap that could be resolved by asking the provider, whereas 'W' indicates a clinical impossibility to determine the status regardless of further questioning. This hierarchy ensures coders prioritize provider clarification before resorting to flags that might artificially inflate POA rates or obscure true acute-onset conditions.

qna.q: How does the rule for conditions diagnosed during admission but suspected prior to admission affect the assignment of 'Y'?
qna.a: This mechanism allows coders to assign 'Y' based on the clinical timeline rather than just the date the code was finalized, recognizing that symptoms present at admission often precede the specific diagnostic label. It prevents the artificial inflation of 'N' (Not Present) rates for chronic conditions that were simply not yet named in the initial admission notes.

qna.q: What are the real-world implications of leaving the POA field blank only for conditions on the 'Exempt from Reporting' list?
qna.a: This restriction forces coders to actively verify if a condition is exempt rather than defaulting to 'N' or 'Y' for every ambiguous case, which directly impacts hospital reimbursement and public health reporting accuracy. By limiting blank fields to specific ICD-10-CM codes, the system reduces data noise while ensuring that reportable conditions are never inadvertently marked as absent.

text:
Assign 'Y' for acute conditions that are present at time of admission and N for acute conditions that are not present at time of admission.

Assign 'Y' for chronic conditions, even though the condition may not be diagnosed until after admission.

If a single code identifies both an acute and chronic condition, see the POA guidelines for codes that contain multiple clinical concepts.
master_label: POA and Chronic Condition Assignment Logic
description: Implements logic to assign 'Y' for acute conditions present at admission and chronic conditions diagnosed post-admission, while referencing guidelines for codes containing multiple clinical concepts.

qna.q: Why does the system assign 'Y' to chronic conditions even when they are only diagnosed after admission?
qna.a: This rule exists to prevent the artificial inflation of hospital-acquired condition metrics, which would occur if a pre-existing chronic issue were misclassified as an infection or complication caused by the stay. By forcing a 'Y' designation for known chronic issues, the coding mechanism ensures that reimbursement penalties and quality scores reflect only conditions that truly originated during the hospitalization.

section_header: Codes That Contain Multiple Clinical Concepts
text:
Assign 'N' if at least one of the clinical concepts included in the code was not present on admission (e.g., COPD with acute exacerbation and the exacerbation was not present on admission; gastric ulcer that does not start bleeding until after admission; asthma patient develops status asthmaticus after admission).

Assign 'Y' if all of the clinical concepts included in the code were present on admission (e.g., duodenal ulcer that perforates prior to admission).

For infection codes that include the causal organism, assign 'Y' if the infection (or signs of the infection) were present on admission, even though the culture results may not be known until after admission (e.g., patient is admitted with pneumonia and the provider documents Pseudomonas as the causal organism a few days later).
master_label: Present on Admission Clinical Assignment Logic
description: Defines the criteria for assigning 'Y' or 'N' values based on whether specific clinical concepts, such as status asthmaticus or ulcers, were present at the time of admission.

qna.q: Why does the rule require assigning 'N' if only *one* clinical concept within a code was not present on admission, rather than requiring all concepts to be absent?
qna.a: This distinction ensures that the code accurately reflects the primary reason for the admission while acknowledging that the condition evolved or worsened after arrival. If a patient is admitted for a stable ulcer but it bleeds later, the bleeding is the acute event driving the stay, so the code must capture that post-admission development to justify the severity level.

qna.q: How does the specific instruction for infection codes handle the timing of culture results versus the presence of clinical signs?
qna.a: The mechanism prioritizes the clinical documentation of the organism's presence at admission over the laboratory confirmation timeline, which often lags behind the initial diagnosis. This prevents the automatic downgrading of infection severity codes simply because the lab took a few days to grow the bacteria, ensuring the code matches the patient's actual risk profile upon entry.

qna.q: What are the real-world implications of assigning 'Y' versus 'N' for a condition like COPD with acute exacerbation?
qna.a: Assigning 'N' signals that the acute exacerbation was the reason for the hospital stay, which typically triggers higher reimbursement rates and specific quality metrics for acute care. Conversely, assigning 'Y' would imply the patient was already in crisis before admission, which might disqualify the claim from certain acute care incentives or suggest a different level of resource utilization.

section_header: Same Diagnosis Code for Two or More Conditions
text:
When the same ICD-10-CM diagnosis code applies to two or more conditions during the same encounter (e.g. two separate conditions classified to the same ICD-10-CM diagnosis code):

Assign 'Y' if all conditions represented by the single ICD-10-CM code were present on admission (e.g. bilateral unspecified age-related cataracts).

Assign 'N' if any of the conditions represented by the single ICD-10-CM code was not present on admission (e.g. traumatic secondary and recurrent hemorrhage and seroma is assigned to a single code T79.2, but only one of the conditions was present on admission).
description: Defines the criteria for assigning 'Y' or 'N' flags when a single ICD-10-CM code represents multiple conditions, based on whether all conditions were present on admission.

qna.q: Why does the rule require assigning 'N' if even a single condition within a shared code was not present on admission?
qna.a: This strict logic prevents the automatic application of 'Y' modifiers to conditions that actually developed during the stay, which would incorrectly inflate the length of stay or trigger unnecessary payment adjustments. By defaulting to 'N' in mixed scenarios, the system ensures that only the specific conditions present at admission are flagged, maintaining accuracy in reimbursement calculations.

qna.q: What are the real-world implications for coding when a single ICD-10-CM code represents both a pre-existing condition and a new complication?
qna.a: In cases like recurrent hemorrhage where one instance is traumatic (new) and another is unspecified, the coder must assign 'N' to the entire code rather than splitting the modifier. This implies that the 'Y' designation cannot be selectively applied to just the pre-existing component, forcing a conservative approach that avoids over-coding conditions that did not exist upon admission.

text:
Whether or not the patient delivers during the current hospitalization does not affect assignment of the POA indicator. The determining factor for POA assignment is whether the pregnancy complication or obstetrical condition described by the code was present at the time of admission or not.

If the pregnancy complication or obstetrical condition was present on admission (e.g., patient admitted in preterm labor), assign 'Y'.

If the pregnancy complication or obstetrical condition was not present on admission (e.g., 2 nd degree laceration during delivery, postpartum hemorrhage that occurred during current hospitalization, fetal distress develops after admission), assign 'N'.

If the obstetrical code includes more than one diagnosis and any of the diagnoses identified by the code were not present on admission assign 'N'.

(e.g., Category O11, Pre-existing hypertension with pre-eclampsia)
master_label: POA Assignment Logic for Obstetrical Conditions
description: Defines the Point of Admission (POA) indicator assignment rules for pregnancy complications based on presence at admission rather than delivery timing. It specifies assigning 'Y' if the condition existed upon admission and 'N' if it developed during the hospitalization.

qna.q: Why does the system ignore whether a patient actually delivers during the hospitalization when assigning the POA indicator?
qna.a: The logic separates the timing of the condition's onset from the outcome of the pregnancy to ensure accurate risk stratification. If the complication existed at admission, it is coded 'Y' regardless of whether the patient later delivers, because the condition itself drove the initial resource utilization and clinical risk.

qna.q: How does the rule requiring 'N' if any diagnosis in a multi-code category was not present on admission affect coding for complex conditions like pre-eclampsia?
qna.a: This mechanism forces a conservative 'N' assignment if even one component of a bundled code (like O11) developed after admission, preventing the entire claim from being flagged as pre-existing. This ensures that only the specific complications truly present at the start of the stay are counted as pre-existing conditions for payment adjustments.

qna.q: What are the real-world implications of assigning 'N' to postpartum hemorrhage or fetal distress that develop during the stay?
qna.a: These conditions will be coded as 'N' because they are acute events occurring after admission rather than pre-existing states, which typically shifts the financial liability to the current episode of care. This distinction prevents the system from incorrectly attributing costs for acute delivery complications to a pre-existing condition modifier.

text: Newborns are not considered to be admitted until after birth. Therefore, any condition present at birth or that developed in utero is considered present at admission and should be assigned 'Y'. This includes conditions that occur during delivery (e.g., injury during delivery, meconium aspiration, exposure to streptococcus B in the vaginal canal).
description: Assigns conditions present at birth or developed in utero, including vaginal exposure to streptococcus, as present at admission for newborns.

qna.q: Why does the system classify conditions present at birth as 'Present on Admission' (POA) for newborns, even though they were not technically admitted to the hospital until after delivery?
qna.a: This rule exists because the clinical reality of a newborn's condition at the moment of birth is functionally identical to a condition present upon admission for an adult patient. By assigning 'Y' to in-utero or delivery-related conditions, the coding mechanism ensures that complications like meconium aspiration are not falsely attributed to the hospital stay, which would otherwise inflate quality metrics and trigger unwarranted penalties.

text: Assign 'Y' for congenital conditions and anomalies except for categories Q00Q99, Congenital anomalies, which are on the exempt list. Congenital conditions are always considered present on admission.
description: Assigns a 'Y' status to congenital conditions and anomalies while excluding categories Q00Q99 and the specific Congenital anomalies exempt list. This logic ensures that qualifying congenital conditions are always marked as present on admission.

qna.q: Why does the system explicitly exclude categories Q00-Q99 from the 'present on admission' flag despite congenital conditions being present at birth?
qna.a: This exclusion likely exists because these specific codes represent the broad category of congenital anomalies themselves, which are inherently present at birth and therefore do not require a separate 'present on admission' designation to be valid. The logic distinguishes between specific congenital conditions (which need the flag to prove they existed before admission) and the general anomaly category (which is self-evident by definition), preventing redundant or logically inconsistent data entry.

text:
Assign 'Y' for any external cause code representing an external cause of morbidity that occurred prior to inpatient admission (e.g., patient fell out of bed at home, patient fell out of bed in emergency room prior to admission)

Assign 'N' for any external cause code representing an external cause of morbidity that occurred during inpatient hospitalization (e.g., patient fell out of hospital bed during hospital stay, patient experienced an adverse reaction to a medication administered after inpatient admission).
description: Assigns 'Y' or 'N' to external cause codes based on whether the event occurred prior to or during inpatient admission.

qna.q: Why is it critical to distinguish between external causes occurring 'prior to admission' versus 'during hospitalization' when assigning Y or N?
qna.a: This distinction separates pre-existing conditions from new complications acquired during care, which directly impacts whether a hospital is financially liable for the event. If a fall happened at home before admission, the hospital isn't responsible for that specific injury, whereas a fall occurring on the ward suggests a potential failure in patient safety protocols.

section_header: ICD-10-CM Official Guidelines for Coding and Reporting
description: Updates the ICD-10-CM guidelines for the 2026 fiscal year to include changes for outpatient coding, perinatal conditions, and congenital disorders originating in the perinatal period.

qna.q: Why do these guidelines explicitly state that classification instructions take precedence over the general guidelines?
qna.a: This hierarchy exists because the Tabular List and Alphabetic Index contain the specific, authoritative definitions for every code, whereas guidelines are interpretive rules meant to resolve ambiguities. If a specific instruction contradicts a general guideline, the specific instruction must win to ensure consistent, legally defensible coding across all healthcare settings.

qna.q: What does the requirement for adherence under HIPAA imply for real-world healthcare operations?
qna.a: It implies that coding is no longer just an administrative best practice but a federal legal mandate with potential financial penalties for non-compliance. Healthcare providers must treat these guidelines as binding regulations to ensure accurate reimbursement and data reporting for the entire US healthcare system.

qna.q: How does the collaboration between CMS, NCHS, AHA, and AHIMA influence the reliability of these coding rules?
qna.a: The involvement of these four distinct Cooperating Parties ensures that the guidelines balance clinical accuracy, administrative feasibility, and statistical utility. This multi-stakeholder approach prevents the rules from being skewed toward a single perspective, making them robust enough for use in diverse settings from hospitals to private practices.

section_header: Section I.  Conventions, general coding guidelines and chapter specific guidelines
text: The conventions, general guidelines and chapter-specific guidelines are applicable to all health care settings unless otherwise indicated. The conventions and instructions of the classification take precedence over guidelines.
description: This implementation establishes that classification conventions and instructions override general and chapter-specific guidelines within all healthcare settings, unless explicitly stated otherwise.

qna.q: Why do conventions take precedence over chapter-specific guidelines when a conflict arises in a healthcare classification?
qna.a: This hierarchy ensures that universal structural rules and data formatting standards remain consistent across all settings, preventing fragmentation caused by localized exceptions. By prioritizing conventions, the system guarantees that the fundamental integrity of the classification data is maintained even when specific clinical guidelines suggest alternative interpretations.

text: The conventions for the ICD-10-CM are the general rules for use of the classification independent of the guidelines. These conventions are incorporated within the Alphabetic Index and Tabular List of the ICD-10-CM as instructional notes.
description: Defines the general rules for using the ICD-10-CM classification system that apply independently of specific guidelines. These conventions are embedded as instructional notes within the Alphabetic Index and Tabular List.

text:
The ICD-10-CM Tabular List contains categories, subcategories and codes. Characters for categories, subcategories and codes may be either a letter or a number. All categories are 3 characters. A three-character category that has no further subdivision is equivalent to a code. Subcategories are either 4 or 5 characters. Codes may be 3, 4, 5, 6 or 7 characters. That is, each level of subdivision after a category is a subcategory. The final level of subdivision is a code. Codes that have applicable 7 th characters are still referred to as codes, not subcategories. A code that has an applicable 7 th character is considered invalid without the 7 th character.

The ICD-10-CM uses an indented format for ease in reference.
description: Defines the hierarchical structure of the ICD-10-CM Tabular List, specifying character lengths for categories, subcategories, and codes up to the final seventh character level.

qna.q: Why is a three-character category treated as a valid code if it has no further subdivision?
qna.a: This design allows the system to accept a broad diagnostic grouping when specific clinical details are unavailable, ensuring a code can always be assigned even without full granularity. It prevents data gaps in reporting while acknowledging that the specificity is lower than a full seven-character code.

qna.q: How does the rule that a code with an applicable seventh character is invalid without it affect data processing?
qna.a: The system must enforce this by rejecting incomplete records or automatically appending the missing character to avoid generating false positives in clinical analysis. This mechanism ensures that critical modifiers, which often dictate treatment or prognosis, are never omitted from the final dataset.

qna.q: What does the distinction between subcategories (4-5 characters) and codes (up to 7 characters) imply for data hierarchy?
qna.a: It implies a multi-level refinement process where each additional character narrows the diagnosis from a general category to a specific clinical scenario. This structure allows analysts to drill down from broad disease groups to precise manifestations without losing the context of the original category.

section_header: 3. Use of codes for reporting purposes
text: For reporting purposes only codes are permissible, not categories or subcategories, and any applicable 7 th character is required.
description: Specifies that only permissible codes are allowed for reporting, excluding categories and subcategories, while mandating the inclusion of the seventh character.

text: The ICD-10-CM utilizes a placeholder character 'X'. The 'X' is used as a placeholder at certain codes to allow for future expansion. An example of this is at the poisoning, adverse effect and underdosing codes, categories T36-T50. Where a placeholder exists, the X must be used in order for the code to be considered a valid code.
description: The ICD-10-CM system utilizes the placeholder character 'X' within categories T36-T50 to enable future code expansion for poisoning, adverse effects, and underdosing.

qna.q: Why does the ICD-10-CM system mandate the use of 'X' as a placeholder in categories like T36-T50 instead of leaving those positions blank or using a different character?
qna.a: The 'X' acts as a structural delimiter that ensures the code string maintains a consistent length and format, which is critical for automated parsing and sorting algorithms. Without this fixed character, legacy systems might misinterpret the code as invalid or fail to distinguish between specific sub-categories during data processing.

text: Certain ICD-10-CM categories have applicable 7 th characters. The applicable 7 th character is required for all codes within the category, or as the notes in the Tabular List instruct. The 7 th character must always be the 7 th character in the data field. If a code that requires a 7 th character is not 6 characters, a placeholder X must be used to fill in the empty characters.
description: Enforces the requirement for applicable 7th characters in ICD-10-CM codes and mandates the use of placeholder 'X' for codes shorter than six characters.

qna.q: Why is a placeholder 'X' required to fill empty characters before the seventh digit instead of simply appending the character to the end?
qna.a: This mechanism ensures the code maintains a fixed length structure that legacy systems and data parsers can reliably validate without complex offset calculations. By forcing the seventh character into the exact seventh position, the system prevents misalignment errors where a missing middle digit might be mistaken for the required status indicator.

text:
- [ ] Brackets are used in the Tabular List to enclose synonyms, alternative wording or explanatory phrases. Brackets are used in the Alphabetic Index to identify manifestation codes.
- ( ) Parentheses are used in both the Alphabetic Index and Tabular List to enclose supplementary words that may be present or absent in the statement of a disease or procedure without affecting the code number to which it is assigned. The terms within the parentheses are referred to as nonessential modifiers. The nonessential modifiers in the Alphabetic Index to Diseases apply to subterms following a main term except when a nonessential modifier and a subentry are mutually exclusive, the subentry takes precedence. For example, in the ICD-10-CM Alphabetic Index under the main term Enteritis, 'acute' is a nonessential modifier and 'chronic' is a subentry. In this case, the nonessential modifier 'acute' does not apply to the subentry 'chronic'.
- : Colons are used in the Tabular List after an incomplete term which needs one or more of the modifiers following the colon to make it assignable to a given category.

'Not elsewhere classifiable'

This abbreviation in the Tabular List represents 'other specified'. When a specific code is not available for a condition, the Tabular List includes an NEC entry under a code to identify the code as the 'other specified' code.

'Not otherwise specified'

This abbreviation is the equivalent of unspecified.

- , Commas are used in the Alphabetic Index and have different meanings based on the context of the Index entry, including alternate verbiage, modifier (essential and nonessential), or alternative for 'and/or.'
description: Defines the usage of brackets and parentheses within the ICD-10-CM Tabular List and Alphabetic Index to distinguish between synonyms and nonessential modifiers.

qna.q: Why does the system treat terms in parentheses as 'nonessential' rather than requiring them for code assignment?
qna.a: These terms are considered nonessential because they describe the condition without changing its fundamental classification or code number. This design allows coders to ignore descriptive details like 'acute' or 'chronic' when looking up a main term, ensuring the search remains efficient even if those specific words are missing from the medical record.

qna.q: How does the rule that subentries take precedence over nonessential modifiers prevent coding errors?
qna.a: The mechanism works by forcing the coder to look past the general descriptor in parentheses to find the specific subentry that defines the unique code. For example, under 'Enteritis', the nonessential modifier 'acute' applies to the general term, but the specific subentry 'chronic' overrides it, ensuring the coder selects the precise code for the chronic condition rather than a generic acute one.

qna.q: What are the real-world implications of confusing nonessential modifiers with essential ones during a code lookup?
qna.a: If a coder mistakenly treats a nonessential modifier as essential, they might fail to find the correct code because the system expects the term to be present for the code to exist. This often leads to selecting an incorrect 'Not elsewhere classifiable' code or missing a specific subentry, resulting in inaccurate billing or statistical reporting.

text: Codes titled 'other' or 'other specified' are for use when the information in the medical record provides detail for which a specific code does not exist. Alphabetic Index entries with NEC in the line designate 'other' codes in the Tabular List. These Alphabetic Index entries represent specific disease entities for which no specific code exists, so the term is included within an 'other' code.
master_label: ICD Code Designation and NEC Index Logic
description: Defines the use of 'other' or 'other specified' codes when specific disease entities lack a dedicated code, utilizing NEC line entries in the Alphabetic Index to designate these categories in the Tabular List.

qna.q: Why does the system require an Alphabetic Index entry with 'NEC' to designate an 'other' code in the Tabular List rather than allowing direct assignment?
qna.a: This mechanism ensures that a specific disease entity is first identified as having no dedicated code before it is forced into a generic 'other' category. It prevents the misuse of broad 'other' codes for conditions that might have been overlooked, maintaining the integrity of the coding hierarchy by requiring a specific search failure first.

text:
Codes titled 'unspecified' are for use when the information in the medical record is insufficient to assign a more specific code. For those categories for which an unspecified code is not provided, the 'other specified' code may represent both other and unspecified.

See Section I.B.18. Use of Signs/Symptom/Unspecified Codes
description: Defines the use of unspecified codes when medical record information is insufficient for specific classification. It clarifies that unspecified codes are reserved for cases where more detailed data is unavailable.

qna.q: Why does the guideline allow 'other specified' codes to represent both 'other' and 'unspecified' categories when specific codes are missing?
qna.a: This flexibility exists to prevent clinical documentation gaps from halting billing or data analysis entirely when a provider cannot yet pinpoint the exact etiology. By allowing a broader category to absorb unspecified cases, the system ensures continuity of care and reporting while still incentivizing providers to gather more specific details over time.

text: This note appears immediately under a three-character code title to further define, or give examples of, the content of the category.
description: This feature implements the display of explanatory notes directly beneath three-character code titles to provide specific definitions or examples for the associated category content.

qna.q: Why is this note positioned immediately under the three-character code title rather than in a separate documentation section?
qna.a: Placing the definition directly beneath the code creates an immediate contextual link, allowing users to verify the meaning of an abstract identifier without navigating away. This proximity reduces cognitive load by ensuring the specific examples or constraints are available exactly when the code is first encountered in a workflow.

text: List of terms is included under some codes. These terms are the conditions for which that code is to be used. The terms may be synonyms of the code title, or, in the case of 'other specified' codes, the terms are a list of the various conditions assigned to that code. The inclusion terms are not necessarily exhaustive. Additional terms found only in the Alphabetic Index may also be assigned to a code.
master_label: ICD-10 Code Inclusion and Index Assignment
description: Defines the list of terms, including synonyms and specific conditions, that are assigned to a code for use in claims processing.

qna.q: Why are inclusion terms described as 'not necessarily exhaustive' and how does this affect real-world coding accuracy?
qna.a: This limitation exists because clinical documentation often evolves or uses specific terminology that hasn't yet been standardized in the index, requiring coders to rely on external Alphabetic Index entries to capture the full clinical picture. In practice, this implies that coders must actively cross-reference multiple sources rather than relying solely on the primary code list to ensure complete and accurate reimbursement.

text: The ICD-10-CM has two types of excludes notes. Each type of note has a different definition for use, but they are all similar in that they indicate that codes excluded from each other are independent of each other.
description: Defines the two distinct types of excludes notes used in ICD-10-CM to indicate that excluded codes are independent of one another.

qna.q: Why does the system distinguish between the two types of excludes notes in ICD-10-CM if they both indicate codes are independent?
qna.a: The distinction exists because one type represents a 'Not Included' relationship where the excluded code is a subset of the included code, while the other represents a 'Rule Out' relationship where the codes are mutually exclusive conditions. This differentiation is critical for clinical accuracy, as it prevents the system from incorrectly assuming a patient has a condition that is explicitly ruled out by a more specific diagnosis.

text:
A type 1 Excludes note is a pure excludes note. It means 'NOT CODED HERE!' An Excludes1 note indicates that the code excluded should never be used at the same time as the code above the Excludes1 note. An Excludes1 is used when two conditions cannot occur together, such as a congenital form versus an acquired form of the same condition.

An exception to the Excludes1 definition is the circumstance when the two conditions are unrelated to each other. If it is not clear whether the two conditions involving an Excludes1 note are related or not, query the provider. For example, code F45.8, Other somatoform disorders, has an Excludes1 note for "sleep related teeth grinding (G47.63)," because "teeth grinding" is an inclusion term under F45.8. Only one of these two codes should be assigned for teeth grinding. However psychogenic dysmenorrhea is also an inclusion term under F45.8, and a patient could have both this condition and sleep related teeth grinding. In this case, the two conditions are clearly unrelated to each other, and so it would be appropriate to report F45.8 and G47.63 together.
description: Defines the Excludes1 note as a pure exclusion indicating that the listed code should never be used simultaneously with the primary code. It clarifies that this rule applies when conditions cannot occur together, such as congenital versus acquired forms, and advises querying the provider if the relationship between conditions is unclear.

qna.q: Why does an Excludes1 note mandate that two codes never be used together, and what clinical scenario justifies this strict exclusion?
qna.a: This restriction exists because the two conditions represent mutually exclusive states of the same pathology, such as a congenital form versus an acquired form. Using both codes would imply a patient simultaneously possesses two biologically impossible versions of the same condition, leading to coding errors.

qna.q: How does the 'unrelated conditions' exception modify the application of an Excludes1 note in practice?
qna.a: The exception allows coders to assign both codes if the conditions are distinct and unrelated, even if one is listed under the other's definition. For instance, while teeth grinding is an inclusion term for somatoform disorders, a patient can independently suffer from psychogenic dysmenorrhea, making the combination valid.

qna.q: What is the real-world implication for coders when the relationship between an Excludes1 condition and a related term is ambiguous?
qna.a: When it is unclear whether two conditions are related or unrelated, the system requires the coder to query the provider for clarification. This prevents the assignment of conflicting codes based on assumptions, ensuring the medical record accurately reflects the patient's specific clinical presentation.

text: A type 2 Excludes note represents 'Not included here.' An excludes2 note indicates that the condition excluded is not part of the condition represented by the code, but a patient may have both conditions at the same time. When an Excludes2 note appears under a code, it is acceptable to use both the code and the excluded code together, when appropriate.
description: Implements the Excludes2 note logic to indicate that an excluded condition is not part of the primary code but may coexist with it. This allows the system to accept both the primary code and the excluded code simultaneously when clinically appropriate.

qna.q: Why does the system distinguish between Excludes1 and Excludes2 notes when determining code validity?
qna.a: The distinction exists because Excludes1 notes represent mutually exclusive conditions where a patient cannot have both, whereas Excludes2 notes allow for co-occurrence. This mechanism ensures that coders do not incorrectly assign conflicting diagnoses while still permitting the documentation of unrelated conditions that happen to share a code.

section_header: 13. Etiology/manifestation convention ('code first', 'use additional code' and 'in diseases classified elsewhere' notes)
text:
Certain conditions have both an underlying etiology and multiple body system manifestations due to the underlying etiology. For such conditions, the ICD-10-CM has a coding convention that requires the underlying condition be sequenced first, if applicable, followed by the manifestation. Wherever such a combination exists, there is a 'use additional code' note at the etiology code, and a 'code first' note at the manifestation code. These instructional notes indicate the proper sequencing order of the codes, etiology followed by manifestation.

In most cases the manifestation codes will have in the code title, 'in diseases classified elsewhere.' Codes with this title are a component of the etiology/ manifestation convention. The code title indicates that it is a manifestation code. 'In diseases classified elsewhere' codes are never permitted to be used as first listed or principal diagnosis codes. They must be used in conjunction with an underlying condition code and they must be listed following the underlying condition. See category F02, Dementia in other diseases classified elsewhere, for an example of this convention.

There are manifestation codes that do not have 'in diseases classified elsewhere' in the title. For such codes, there is a 'use additional code' note at the etiology code and a 'code first' note at the manifestation code, and the rules for sequencing apply.

In addition to the notes in the Tabular List, these conditions also have a specific Alphabetic Index entry structure. In the Alphabetic Index both conditions are listed together with the etiology code first followed by the manifestation codes in brackets. The code in brackets is always to be sequenced second.

An example of the etiology/manifestation convention is dementia with Parkinson's disease. In the Alphabetic Index, a code from category G20 is listed first, followed by code F02.80 or F02.81 -in brackets. A code from category G20 -represents the underlying etiology, Parkinson's disease, and must be sequenced first, whereas codes F02.80 and F02.81 -represent the manifestation of dementia in diseases classified elsewhere, with or without behavioral disturbance.

'Code first' and 'Use additional code' notes are also used as sequencing rules in the classification for certain codes that are not part of an etiology/ manifestation combination.

See Section I.B.7. Multiple coding for a single condition.
description: Implements the ICD-10-CM coding convention requiring underlying etiology codes to be sequenced first, followed by manifestation codes with 'in diseases classified elsewhere' titles.

qna.q: Why does the ICD-10-CM convention require the underlying etiology to be sequenced before the manifestation code?
qna.a: This sequencing rule exists because the manifestation is clinically dependent on the specific underlying disease; without identifying the root cause first, the clinical picture is incomplete. The 'code first' instruction at the manifestation code enforces this logic, ensuring that the principal diagnosis reflects the primary pathology driving the symptoms.

qna.q: How does the presence of 'in diseases classified elsewhere' in a code title function as a mechanism to prevent coding errors?
qna.a: The title acts as a built-in flag that explicitly forbids the code from being used as a principal diagnosis, forcing the coder to look up the associated etiology in the Alphabetic Index. This structural constraint prevents the common error of listing a symptom or secondary effect as the primary reason for admission when a specific disease is present.

qna.q: What does the specific Alphabetic Index entry structure imply for the workflow of a coder encountering a complex condition like dementia with Parkinson's disease?
qna.a: The index listing the etiology first followed by manifestation codes in brackets implies a direct lookup path where the coder must select the primary disease code before the secondary manifestation code. This structure guides the user to the correct sequencing order immediately, reducing the cognitive load of cross-referencing tabular notes during the initial coding phase.

text:
The word 'and' should be interpreted to mean either 'and' or 'or' when it appears in a title.

For example, cases of 'tuberculosis of bones', 'tuberculosis of joints' and 'tuberculosis of bones and joints' are classified to subcategory A18.0, Tuberculosis of bones and joints.
description: Defines the interpretation of the word 'and' in titles to classify tuberculosis cases of bones, joints, or both into subcategory A18.0.

qna.q: Why does the system interpret 'and' in a title as 'or' when classifying tuberculosis cases?
qna.a: This change likely addresses a semantic ambiguity where a title like 'tuberculosis of bones and joints' implies a combined condition, but clinically represents distinct sites of infection that should be grouped under a single subcategory. By treating 'and' as 'or', the engine ensures that cases involving either bone or joint involvement (or both) are consistently routed to the correct A18.0 subcategory without requiring separate rule entries for every possible combination.

text:
The word 'with' or 'in' should be interpreted to mean 'associated with' or 'due to' when it appears in a code title, the Alphabetic Index (either under a main term or subterm), or an instructional note in the Tabular List. The classification presumes a causal relationship between the two conditions linked by these terms in the Alphabetic Index or Tabular List. These conditions should be coded as related even in the absence of provider documentation explicitly linking them, unless the documentation clearly states the conditions are unrelated or when another guideline exists that specifically

requires a documented linkage between two conditions (e.g., sepsis guideline for 'acute organ dysfunction that is not clearly associated with the sepsis'). documented, provider documentation must link the conditions in order to code them as

For conditions not specifically linked by these relational terms in the classification or when a guideline requires that a linkage between two conditions be explicitly related.

The word 'with' in the Alphabetic Index is sequenced immediately following the main term or subterm, not in alphabetical order.
description: Defines the interpretation of terms like 'with' or 'in' to presume a causal relationship between conditions in the Alphabetic Index and Tabular List. This rule mandates coding related conditions without explicit provider documentation unless specific guidelines require documented linkage.

qna.q: Why does the system presume a causal relationship between conditions linked by 'with' or 'in' even without explicit provider documentation?
qna.a: This presumption prevents under-coding of complex clinical scenarios where the causal link is clinically obvious but not explicitly written in the chart. By treating these terms as definitive indicators of association, the system ensures that comorbidities are captured accurately without forcing clinicians to add redundant notes for every logical connection.

qna.q: How does the specific sequencing of the word 'with' in the Alphabetic Index affect code selection compared to standard alphabetical sorting?
qna.a: Placing 'with' immediately after the main term ensures that codes for conditions associated with a primary issue appear directly under that term, streamlining the lookup process for coders. This structural choice prioritizes the clinical relationship over alphabetical order, making it faster to find the correct code for a specific complication or manifestation.

qna.q: What are the real-world implications for coding accuracy when guidelines require explicit documentation despite the general presumption of causality?
qna.a: In cases like sepsis-related organ dysfunction, the system will not automatically code the relationship unless the provider explicitly documents the link, preventing over-coding of unrelated conditions. This exception ensures that the classification remains precise and legally defensible by strictly adhering to the specific evidence required for high-stakes causal claims.

text:
The 'see' instruction following a main term in the Alphabetic Index indicates that another term should be referenced. It is necessary to go to the main term referenced with the 'see' note to locate the correct code.

A 'see also' instruction following a main term in the Alphabetic Index instructs that there is another main term that may also be referenced that may provide additional Alphabetic Index entries that may be useful. It is not necessary to follow the 'see also' note when the original main term provides the necessary code.
description: Defines the logic for interpreting 'see' and 'see also' instructions within the Alphabetic Index to locate correct main terms and codes.

qna.q: Why does the system require following a 'see' instruction to find the correct code rather than providing the code directly under the initial term?
qna.a: This design forces the user to navigate to the primary term where the official definition and coding criteria are located, ensuring that the selected code matches the specific clinical context. Directly listing codes under synonyms risks ambiguity, as a single term might map to multiple codes depending on the specific condition or severity described.

qna.q: What is the practical implication of treating 'see also' instructions as optional rather than mandatory during code selection?
qna.a: Treating 'see also' as optional allows coders to stop immediately once they find the code that fits the patient's condition without needing to cross-reference additional terms. This prevents unnecessary navigation time and reduces the risk of selecting an incorrect code simply because a related term was checked out of habit rather than necessity.

text: A 'code also' note instructs that two codes may be required to fully describe a condition, but this note does not provide sequencing direction. The sequencing depends on the circumstances of the encounter.
master_label: Code Sequencing Logic for Encounter Notes
description: This logic implements conditional sequencing for 'code also' notes by deferring the order of two required codes to the specific circumstances of the encounter, as the note itself lacks inherent sequencing direction.

qna.q: Why does the 'code also' note for congenital sepsis lack specific sequencing direction, and how does this affect real-world coding decisions?
qna.a: The absence of a fixed sequence implies that the coder must analyze the specific clinical circumstances of the encounter to determine which condition was primary or more significant. This requires a deeper review of the medical record rather than a simple lookup, ensuring the final code assignment accurately reflects the patient's actual presentation.

text: A code listed next to a main term in the ICD-10-CM Alphabetic Index is referred to as a default code. The default code represents that condition that is most commonly associated with the main term or is the unspecified code for the condition. If a condition is documented in a medical record (for example, appendicitis) without any additional information, such as acute or chronic, the default code should be assigned.
description: Defines the use of default codes from the ICD-10-CM Alphabetic Index when a condition is documented without additional modifiers like acute or chronic.

qna.q: Why does the ICD-10-CM system rely on default codes when a condition is documented without specific qualifiers like 'acute' or 'chronic'?
qna.a: This mechanism ensures that every documented condition receives a valid, billable code even when clinical documentation is incomplete. By assigning the most common or unspecified code, the system prevents data gaps that would otherwise require manual intervention or result in claim denials.

text: The assignment of a diagnosis code is based on the provider's diagnostic statement that the condition exists. The provider's statement that the patient has a particular condition is sufficient. Code assignment is not based on clinical criteria used by the provider to establish the diagnosis. If there is conflicting medical record documentation, query the provider.
master_label: Code Assignment and Diagnosis Exception Logic
description: Defines that diagnosis code assignment relies solely on the provider's statement that a condition exists, without requiring additional clinical criteria. Conflicting documentation requires a provider query to resolve discrepancies.

qna.q: Why does the system prioritize the provider's explicit statement over clinical criteria when assigning diagnosis codes?
qna.a: This approach ensures legal and billing accuracy by treating the provider's documented assertion as the definitive source of truth for reimbursement purposes. It prevents the system from making subjective clinical judgments that could lead to denials if the provider's intent differs from standard diagnostic algorithms.

section_header: 1. Locating a code in the ICD-10-CM
text:
To select a code in the classification that corresponds to a diagnosis or reason for visit documented in a medical record, first locate the term in the Alphabetic Index, and then verify the code in the Tabular List. Read and be guided by instructional notations that appear in both the Alphabetic Index and the Tabular List.

It is essential to use both the Alphabetic Index and Tabular List when locating and assigning a code. The Alphabetic Index does not always provide the full code. Selection of the full code, including laterality and any applicable 7 th character can only be done in the Tabular List. A dash (-) at the end of an Alphabetic Index entry indicates that additional characters are required. Even if a dash is not included at the Alphabetic Index entry, it is necessary to refer to the Tabular List to verify that no 7 th character is required.
description: Defines the process of locating and assigning diagnosis codes by cross-referencing the Alphabetic Index and Tabular List to ensure accurate code selection with full specificity.

qna.q: Why is it strictly necessary to verify codes in the Tabular List even when the Alphabetic Index entry lacks a dash?
qna.a: The Alphabetic Index is designed as a starting point that often omits critical details like laterality or required 7th characters to save space. Relying solely on the absence of a dash could lead to assigning an incomplete code, so the Tabular List acts as the mandatory verification step to ensure the code fully captures the clinical documentation.

qna.q: How does the presence of a dash in the Alphabetic Index change the coder's workflow compared to an entry without one?
qna.a: A dash explicitly signals that the code is incomplete and requires additional characters, forcing the coder to immediately consult the Tabular List for the full selection. Without a dash, the coder must still check the Tabular List to confirm that no hidden requirements exist, making the dash a visual cue rather than a definitive rule for completeness.

qna.q: What are the real-world implications of failing to follow the instruction to read instructional notations in both lists?
qna.a: Ignoring notations in either list can result in assigning a code that does not accurately reflect the patient's condition, such as missing a laterality modifier or a severity indicator. This leads to claim denials or incorrect reimbursement because the submitted code fails to match the specific clinical scenario documented in the medical record.

text:
Diagnosis codes are to be used and reported at their highest number of characters available and to the highest level of specificity documented in the medical record.

ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes are composed of codes with 3, 4, 5, 6 or 7 characters. Codes with three characters are included in ICD-10-CM as the heading of a category of codes that may be further subdivided by the use of fourth and/or fifth characters and/or sixth characters, which provide greater detail.

A three-character code is to be used only if it is not further subdivided. A code is invalid if it has not been coded to the full number of characters required for that code, including the 7 th character, if applicable.
description: Mandates the use of diagnosis codes at their highest character count and level of specificity as documented in the medical record. Ensures codes are invalid if not fully expanded to the required number of characters, including the seventh character when applicable.

qna.q: Why does the system enforce a penalty for codes that stop at three characters when a fourth or fifth character exists?
qna.a: This rule exists because the first three characters represent a broad category rather than a specific diagnosis, which can lead to inaccurate billing and clinical data. Using the full code ensures the medical record reflects the precise condition documented, preventing under-coding that might result in claim denials or loss of quality metrics.

qna.q: How does the requirement to use the seventh character affect the real-world assignment of injury codes?
qna.a: The seventh character indicates the episode of care (e.g., initial encounter vs. sequela), so the system must validate that this specific modifier is included for any applicable injury code. Omitting it renders the code invalid because it fails to distinguish between the acute phase of an injury and its long-term complications, which are treated differently for reimbursement and treatment planning.

section_header: 3. Code or codes from A00.0 through T88.9, Z00-Z99.8, U00-U85
text: The appropriate code or codes from A00.0 through T88.9, Z00-Z99.8, and U00-U85 must be used to identify diagnoses, symptoms, conditions, problems, complaints or other reason(s) for the encounter/visit.
description: Specifies the required use of codes from ranges A00.0 through T88.9, Z00-Z99.8, and U00-U85 to identify diagnoses and symptoms for an encounter visit.

text:
Codes that describe symptoms and signs, as opposed to diagnoses, are acceptable for reporting purposes when a related definitive diagnosis has not been established (confirmed) by the provider. Chapter 18 of ICD-10-CM, Symptoms, Signs, and Abnormal Clinical and Laboratory Findings, Not Elsewhere Classified (codes R00.0 - R99) contains many, but not all, codes for symptoms.

See Section I.B.18. Use of Signs/Symptom/Unspecified Codes
master_label: Symptom and Sign Code Reporting Guidelines
description: Establishes that ICD-10-CM codes for symptoms and signs (Chapter 18, R00.0-R99) are acceptable for reporting when a definitive diagnosis has not yet been confirmed by the provider.

qna.q: Why does the system allow symptom codes like R00.0-R99 to be reported when a definitive diagnosis hasn't been confirmed yet?
qna.a: This change ensures that valid clinical data isn't lost during the diagnostic process, allowing providers to document the patient's current state even before a specific disease is identified. It prevents the need for placeholder codes or delays in reporting, ensuring the medical record accurately reflects the patient's presentation at the time of service.

section_header: 5. Conditions that are an integral part of a disease process
text: Signs and symptoms that are associated routinely with a disease process should not be assigned as additional codes, unless otherwise instructed by the classification.
master_label: ICD-10-CM Coding Guidelines for Signs and Symptoms
description: Establishes that signs and symptoms routinely associated with a disease process must not be coded as additional diagnoses unless specific classification instructions dictate otherwise.

qna.q: Why does the classification system explicitly forbid coding routine signs and symptoms as additional codes?
qna.a: This restriction prevents 'code inflation' by ensuring that only the primary disease process drives the diagnosis, rather than listing every minor symptom that naturally accompanies it. By suppressing these routine findings, the system maintains data integrity for epidemiological tracking and ensures that treatment decisions focus on the underlying pathology rather than incidental observations.

section_header: 6. Conditions that are not an integral part of a disease process
text: Additional signs and symptoms that may not be associated routinely with a disease process should be coded when present.
description: This passage implements the requirement to code additional signs and symptoms that are not routinely associated with a disease process when they are present in the patient record. It ensures that these specific clinical findings are captured as distinct codes rather than being omitted or subsumed under the primary diagnosis.

section_header: 7. Multiple coding for a single condition
text:
In addition to the etiology/manifestation convention that requires two codes to fully describe a single condition that affects multiple body systems, there are other single conditions that also require more than one code. 'Use additional code' notes are found in the Tabular List at codes that are not part of an etiology/manifestation pair where a secondary code is useful to fully describe a condition. The sequencing rule is the same as the etiology/manifestation pair, 'use additional code' indicates that a secondary code should be added , if known.

For example, for bacterial infections that are not included in chapter 1, a secondary code from category B95, Streptococcus, Staphylococcus, and Enterococcus, as the cause of diseases classified elsewhere, or B96, Other bacterial agents as the cause of diseases classified elsewhere, may be required to identify the bacterial organism causing the infection. A 'use additional code' note will normally be found at the infectious disease code, indicating a need for the organism code to be added as a secondary code.

'Code first' notes are also under certain codes that are not specifically manifestation codes but may be due to an underlying cause. When there is a 'code first' note and an underlying condition is present, the underlying condition should be sequenced first, if known.

'Code, if applicable, any causal condition first' notes indicate that this code may be assigned as a principal diagnosis when the causal condition is unknown or not applicable. If a causal condition is known, then the code for that condition should be sequenced as the principal or first-listed diagnosis.

Multiple codes may be needed for sequela, complication codes and obstetric codes to more fully describe a condition. See the specific guidelines for these conditions for further instruction.
description: Defines sequencing guidelines requiring additional codes to fully describe single conditions affecting multiple body systems or bacterial infections. The rules mandate using secondary codes from specific categories when 'use additional code' notes are present in the Tabular List.

qna.q: Why does the system require a secondary code for bacterial organisms even when the primary infection code already exists?
qna.a: This requirement exists because the primary infection code often describes the clinical syndrome rather than the specific pathogen, which is critical for tracking antibiotic resistance and epidemiological trends. Without the secondary organism code, the data lacks the granularity needed to distinguish between, for example, a generic 'bacterial pneumonia' and one specifically caused by a resistant strain of Staphylococcus.

qna.q: How does the 'Code first' note alter the sequencing logic compared to standard 'Use additional code' notes?
qna.a: While 'Use additional code' simply mandates adding a secondary detail, 'Code first' actively reverses the default sequencing rule by forcing the underlying etiology to be listed as the principal diagnosis. This ensures that the root cause of the condition drives the statistical reporting and resource allocation, rather than the symptomatic manifestation.

qna.q: What are the real-world implications of the 'Code, if applicable, any causal condition first' note for unknown cases?
qna.a: This rule allows clinicians to assign a specific complication or sequela code as the principal diagnosis when the root cause is currently unknown, ensuring the patient's immediate clinical status is captured accurately. It prevents the need to leave the principal diagnosis blank or use a generic placeholder, maintaining data integrity until the causal condition is identified.

text: If the same condition is described as both acute (subacute) and chronic, and separate subentries exist in the Alphabetic Index at the same indentation level, code both and sequence the acute (subacute) code first.
master_label: Acute and Chronic Condition Coding Rules
description: Establishes coding guidelines for conditions described as both acute and chronic when separate subentries exist at the same index indentation level. Requires sequencing the acute or subacute code first when both are assigned.

qna.q: Why does the coding sequence prioritize acute conditions over chronic ones when both are present in the same encounter?
qna.a: This sequencing rule reflects the clinical reality that acute conditions often represent the primary reason for the current medical encounter or require immediate intervention. By coding the acute condition first, the medical record accurately highlights the most urgent clinical issue, which is critical for determining the primary diagnosis for reimbursement and treatment planning.

text:
A combination code is a single code used to classify: Two diagnoses, or A diagnosis with an associated secondary process (manifestation) A diagnosis with an associated complication

Combination codes are identified by referring to subterm entries in the Alphabetic Index and by reading the inclusion and exclusion notes in the Tabular List.

Assign only the combination code when that code fully identifies the diagnostic conditions involved or when the Alphabetic Index so directs. Multiple coding should not be used when the classification provides a combination code that clearly identifies all of the elements documented in the diagnosis. When the combination code lacks necessary specificity in describing the manifestation or complication, an additional code should be used as a secondary code.
description: Defines the rules for assigning single combination codes that identify diagnoses with associated complications or manifestations. It specifies when to use a single code versus adding a secondary code based on the specificity of the classification.

qna.q: Why does the guideline explicitly forbid using multiple codes when a single combination code fully identifies the conditions?
qna.a: This restriction prevents unnecessary coding complexity and ensures that the medical record reflects the most specific single diagnosis available. Using multiple codes in this scenario would fragment the clinical picture, making it harder to analyze the primary relationship between the disease and its manifestation or complication.

qna.q: How does the requirement to check inclusion and exclusion notes impact the accuracy of assigning combination codes?
qna.a: These notes act as a critical filter to determine if a specific condition is validly included in the combination code or if it must be coded separately.
